Operator Briefing & Signatures
I understand the risk assessments and control measures. I have received information regarding Fire, Medical Emergency and Accident Reporting. I declare that I am fit to take part in work.
Scope and Limitations This check sheet supports basic due diligence for trained arborists during daytime tree works only. It does not replace a professional ecological survey. If Schedule 1 species are suspected, or works are planned during darkness, a licensed ecologist must be consulted before works commence.
Legal Obligations Under the Wildlife & Countryside Act 1981, it is an offence to intentionally take, damage or destroy the nest of any wild bird while in use or being built. All operatives must comply prior to and throughout all works.
Work Details
Quote Ref
Date of Inspection
Site Location
Inspector Name
Project Scope
Nesting Season Awareness
Jan · Sep · Oct · Nov · Dec
Lower RiskSome species remain active year-round. An inspection is still required before work commences.
Feb · Aug
Elevated RiskEarly nesters or late broods may be active. A careful inspection is strongly advised.
Mar · Apr · May · Jun · Jul
Peak Nesting SeasonThese months fall within the primary nesting period. A thorough inspection is mandatory before any work begins.

⚠ Emergency Procedure

ATS Ltd Emergency Preparedness and Response Procedure V1 · 25.09.2025
Reference: ATS Ltd Emergency Preparedness and Response Procedure V1 — 25.09.2025
Emergency Contact Numbers
Environment Agency (24hr)0800 80 70 60
Floodline (24hr)0345 988 1188
National Grid (Gas)0800 111 999
National Grid Plant Protection0800 688 588
HSE Incident Contact Centre0845 300 9923
Arb Matters (HSEQ Advisors)07879 658047
Nationwide Platforms0345 745 0000
Emergency Services999 / 112
Accident Reporting Procedure
Accident occurs
▼
Injury sustained
▼
Immediate first aid or medical attention given
▼
Reporting of event via company reporting system
► 
► Contact the main office ASAP
► Office shall determine and make contact with next of kin if required
▼
Accident book completed / Incident log updated
Person reporting? Injured/Involved? RIDDOR reportable? Corrective action? Root cause? Immediate cause? Preventative action?
▼
Analysis of data undertaken
▼
RIDDOR report made
Changes to work practices, update information provided to operators
Update/refresher training initiated
Review of policies and/or procedures
▼
Management review
Spill Procedure
Spill occurs
▼
Think Safety
Do you need Protective Clothing? Is there a risk of fire? Is machinery stable? Evacuate all people to a safe muster point upwind of the spillage.
▼
Deal with Injuries
Check people involved for injuries. Treat as priority. Remove them upwind if safe. If reacted to substance, take label to doctor. Call emergency services if necessary.
▼
Stop the Spillage
Stop the source or contain any further spillage. Call for assistance if necessary.
▼
Contain the Spillage
Keep the spillage as small an area as possible by blocking drains, making dams and using absorbent materials including soil.
▼
Clean up the Spillage
Use proper absorbent materials to soak up the spill. Used Spill Kits etc. must be properly disposed of.
▼
Inform and Record
Contact the main office ASAP. Contamination of watercourses where there is a requirement to inform organisations such as the EA, will be dealt with by our office. Complete and submit an adverse event report form.
Fire Procedure
Fire occurs
▼
Think Safety
Evacuate all people to a safe upwind position.
▼
Raise the Alarm
Call 999 or 112 using the nearest telephone. Ask for the fire brigade. Using the Emergency Information on the site paperwork, provide as much information as you can to help assist in providing direction and location.
▼
Fire Fighting
Only fight a fire when it is appropriate to do so, and an individual is not placing themselves in greater danger. Remember PASS — Pull the pin, Aim the hose, Squeeze the lever, Sweep the hose. Put people first, and property second.
▼
Inform and Record
Contact the main office ASAP. Complete and submit an incident report form.
Medical Emergency Procedure
Medical Emergency Occurs
▼
Trained site First Aiders to administer First Aid and assess condition of casualty
▼
Only if it is safe and appropriate to do so, move the casualty take them to the nearest appropriate hospital
▼
If necessary, call 999 or 112 using the nearest telephone. Using the Emergency Information on the site paperwork, provide as much information as you can to help assist in providing direction and location along with details of the casualty
▼
Contact the main office ASAP
Office shall determine and make contact with next of kin if required
▼
Complete and submit an accident report form
Contact with Utilities Procedure
Contact with utilities
▼
Do not touch any exposed or damaged utility, do not attempt to untangle any form of wire or cable from machinery
▼
Do not attempt any form of repair
▼
Do not smoke or allow naked flames
▼
Assume that any wires are live, even if they are not arcing or sparking, or if they otherwise appear to be dead
▼
Remember that, even if lines are dead, they may be switched back on either automatically after a few seconds or remotely after a few minutes or even hours if the line's owner is not aware that their line has been damaged
▼
Contact the relevant service national emergency number immediately to inform them of what has happened
▼
If required, call the emergency services. Give them your location, tell them what has happened and that utilities are involved
▼
If you are in contact with, or close to, a damaged service, move away as quickly as possible and stay away until the service owner advises that the situation has been made safe
▼
If you are in a vehicle that has touched a wire, either stay in the vehicle or, if you need to get out, jump out of it as far as you can
▼
Do not touch the vehicle while standing on the ground. Do not return to the vehicle until it has been confirmed that it is safe to do so
▼
Be aware that if a live wire is touching the ground the area around it may be live. Keep a safe distance away from the wire or anything else it may be touching and keep others away
▼
Ensure that no other third party can enter the immediate vicinity, and any person affected by the event are provided with a warning
▼
Report all damage even where leaks or loss of power etc may not be evident
▼
Complete and submit an incident report form
Road Traffic Collision Procedure
Road Traffic Collision occurs
▼
Stop the Vehicle
Please remember it is against the law to drive away following an accident. Turn off the engine. Switch the hazard lights on.
▼
Check for Injuries
Check for any injuries to yourself or your passengers. If it's a minor collision and there are no injuries, make a note of it just in case. Call the police and an ambulance immediately if anyone is hurt or if the road is blocked.
▼
Remain Calm
Try to remain as calm as possible — it's normal to be shaken after an accident. Take a few deep breaths and try to take stock of the situation the best you can without losing your temper.
▼
Exchange Details
Exchange details with anyone involved in the accident (including witnesses). Get their: a. Name, b. Address, c. Telephone number, d. Vehicle registration number.
▼
Contact the main office
Contact next of kin if required. Arrange for roadside assistance/tow. Complete incident report form. Contact insurers if applicable. Do not apologise or admit responsibility for the crash until you're completely aware of what happened.
Contact with Underground Services Procedure
Contact with Underground Services
▼
Stop work immediately: Cease all excavation and any interaction with the damaged service.
▼
Do not approach: Do not touch, move, or try to fix the damaged service.
▼
Report the incident: Contact the main office, and/or the relevant utility provider or emergency services. The priority of contact is dependent upon the severity of underground contact — for example if contact was made with a gas main it should be the utility provider who is immediately called.
▼
Do not resume work: You cannot restart the work until the service owner, declare the area safe and issue an amended permit (where issued).
▼
Protect the damaged service: Ensure the damaged area is protected from further damage or contact until it is safe to proceed, ensure site cordons/exclusion zones remain in place.
Dealing with Threatening Behaviour Procedure
Confronted with Threatening Behaviour
▼
Identification of behaviour
Observe: Aggressive language, verbal abuse, intimidation, physical threats, hostile actions.
▼
Stay calm and assess the risk
Remain composed and avoid escalation. You need to quickly assess if there is an immediate risk of harm.
▼
Is there immediate danger present?
Where you believe there is an immediate risk of harm, or escalation that you will not be able to control — call 999. Remove yourself and others to safety.
▼
No immediate danger, but behaviour is threatening
Use de-escalation techniques; calm tone, non-confrontational stance, active listening. If behaviour continues, request the person to stop or leave the area. If unresolved or you believe you are at risk of harm, call 999 (Police).
▼
Report the incident
All interactions where threatening behaviour has been encountered must be reported to the office. Complete incident report form including event details. You may be required to speak to the Police. Further support will be offered to those affected as required.
Aerial Tree Rescue Procedure
Aerial Rescue/Assistance Required
▼
Stop work, assess the situation and determine whether emergency services are required
▼
Use of personal fall protection equipment
Determine if self-rescue is possible. If possible, allow self-rescue to take place.
Use of MEWP for rescue
Deploy MEWP. If climber can communicate & is mobile: facilitate own rescue. If unable to communicate: MEWP operator attaches casualty to platform, then attaches fall protection, then disengages. Descent made.
MEWP to MEWP
Deploy second rescue MEWP close together with access doors open. Operator attaches to rescue MEWP prior to disconnecting from their own. Descent made.
Broken down / unable to descend MEWP
Lower using ground controls, emergency power pack or manual bleed down systems. If available, use another MEWP or tree structure/personal fall protection. Contact service engineer/hire company.
▼
Deal with the aftermath:
a. Contact the main office
b. Complete accident/incident report forms
c. Quarantine the site/preserve the scene as necessary
Definitions
Accident
Any unplanned event that resulted in injury or ill health of people.
Incident
Damage or loss to property, plant, materials or the environment.
Near Miss
Any event which, under slightly different circumstances, may have resulted in injury or ill health of people or damage/loss to property, plant, materials or the environment.

Tree Surgery PPE & Safety Guide

Based on HSE L25 — Personal Protective Equipment at Work Regulations 1992 — Arborite Tree Services Ltd
⚠ Under the PPE at Work Regulations 1992 (Regulation 4), every employer MUST provide suitable PPE to employees exposed to risk. Employees MUST use it correctly (Regulation 10). Failure is a legal offence.
📄 Legal Framework — PPE at Work Regulations 1992 (L25)
Regulation 4: Employer must provide suitable PPE where risk cannot be controlled by other means
Regulation 5: Multiple PPE items must be compatible with each other
Regulation 6: PPE must be assessed as suitable before provision
Regulation 7: PPE must be maintained, cleaned and replaced as needed
Regulation 8: Appropriate storage/accommodation must be provided for PPE
Regulation 9: Employees must receive information, instruction and training
Regulation 10: Employees must use PPE properly as instructed
Regulation 11: Employees must report any loss or defect immediately
Note: PPE is the LAST resort — engineering controls and safe systems of work must be considered first (L25 Guidance 4, para 26)
📄 PPE Requirements by Work Activity
ⓘ Note: ✓ = Required  |  — = Not applicable  |  Text = Condition applies. All PPE must comply with listed BS/EN standards under the Personal Protective Equipment at Work Regulations 1992.
EquipmentBS / EN StandardUsing a chainsaw on the groundUsing a chainsaw off the groundPlant operatorMachine operator (woodchipper, stumpgrinder)Brushcutter / trimmer
Safety helmetBS EN 397 / BS EN 12492✓✓To be worn outside of a protective cab✓✓
Hearing protectionBS EN 352-1✓✓—✓✓
Eye protection mesh visorBS EN 1731✓✓—✓✓
Safety glasses or gogglesBS EN 166✓✓—As determined by SSRA✓
GlovesSuitable for intended task✓No mandatory requirement—Non-snag/close fitting✓
Leg and groin protectionBS EN 381-5✓✓—Non-snag/close fitting outer clothing including long trousersNon-snag/close fitting outer clothing including long trousers
Chainsaw bootsBS EN ISO 17249✓✓Steel toe cappedSteel toe cappedSteel toe capped
High visibility clothing The use of high visibility clothing is to be implemented following the undertaking of a suitable and sufficient site-specific risk assessment. When worn, it must be ensured items are close fitting and non-snag, meeting the requirements of BS EN ISO 20471.
⚠ Section 1 — Head Protection (L25 para 86–88)
Industrial Safety Helmet MANDATORY
Standard: EN 397 / EN 12492
  • Worn during ALL tree work — mandatory (L25 para 87c: tree felling)
  • Use adjustable chinstrap — helmet must not fall off (para 88a)
  • Replace every 3 years or after any significant impact (para 88d)
  • Do NOT customise, paint or drill holes (para 88g)
  • Worn with brim level — never back-to-front (para 88e–f)
Employer duty: Provide & maintain. Employee duty: Wear correctly, report defects (Reg 10–11)
Climbing Helmet (EN 12492) CLIMBING
Standard: EN 12492 — climbing/mountaineering helmet
  • Designed for side and top impact — essential for aerial work
  • Must be fitted with chinstrap — worn and fastened when climbing
  • Compatible with visor and ear defenders (Reg 5 — compatibility)
  • Check suspension harness inside before every use
  • Retire immediately after any significant impact
👁 Section 2 — Eye & Face Protection (L25 para 89–91)
Mesh Visor (Chainsaw) MANDATORY
Standard: EN 1731 — mesh face shield
  • Required for ALL chainsaw operation — flying debris risk (para 90b)
  • Check mesh — no blocked, bent or broken sections before use
  • Clean regularly — obscured vision increases risk
  • Must be compatible with helmet and ear defenders (Reg 5)
  • Issue on personal basis — store in protective case (para 91c)
Safety Glasses / Goggles
Standard: EN 166 — eye protection for industrial use
  • Required for chipper operation and stump grinding
  • Anti-scratch and anti-fog lenses required
  • Discard if lenses are deeply scratched or frame distorted (para 91e)
  • Goggles provide full rim contact — best for dust and debris
  • Faceshields protect face but do not fully enclose eyes (para 89c)
🤸 Section 3 — Hand & Arm Protection (L25 para 92–94)
Chainsaw Gloves MANDATORY
Standard: EN 381-7 Class 1 minimum (para 92, L25 p.31†)
  • Cut-resistant protection on back of left hand — minimum
  • Must fit snugly — no loose material near chain (para 94b)
  • Check cut-resistant padding layers before each use
  • Retire if protection layer is damaged or cut (para 94e)
  • Keep hands warm in cold — reduces vibration white finger risk (para 93c)
General Work Gloves
Standard: EN 388 — mechanical risk gloves
  • Worn during climbing, rigging and manual handling
  • Check for holes, cuts and worn areas before use (para 94e)
  • Do not wear for extended periods — skin moisture risk (para 94f)
  • Check for latex allergy before issuing rubber gloves (para 94a)
  • Fit correctly — no gap at wrist (para 94b)
🛠 Section 4 — Protective Clothing & Legs (L25 para 95–98, p.31†)
Chainsaw Trousers MANDATORY
Standard: EN 381-5 Class 1 (20 m/s) — L25 p.31†
  • Front leg protection (Type A) minimum for ground work
  • All-round (Type C) required for climbing — most protective
  • Anti-clog synthetic fibre layers clogs chainsaw sprocket on contact
  • Inspect seams, surface damage before use (para 98d)
  • Do not store contaminated trousers with clean PPE (para 98a)
  • Retire immediately if chain contact occurs — even no visible damage
Weatherproof Clothing
Covered by Regulations where worn to manage health risk (Reg 3 — Clothing)
  • Waterproof/insulated clothing is PPE if worn against health/safety risks
  • Protects against cold and wet — hypothermia risk management
  • Do not wear loose clothing near moving machinery (para 98e)
  • Employer must provide; employee must not be charged (Reg 4, para 37)
🔳 Section 5 — High Visibility Clothing (L25 para 99–102)
Hi-Vis Vest / Jacket MANDATORY
Standard: EN ISO 20471 — Class 2 min, Class 3 near traffic (para 101b)
  • Fluorescent yellow/orange background with retroflective bands (para 99)
  • Class 3 (full sleeves) — dual carriageways 50mph+ (para 101b)
  • Inspect for wear, fading and loose seams before use (para 102b)
  • Clean using correct materials — dirt reduces visibility (para 102c)
  • Worn over all other PPE — always visible to others
👟 Section 6 — Foot Protection (L25 para 103–105)
Safety Boots (Chainsaw) MANDATORY
Standard: EN ISO 20345 S3 + EN 381-3 chainsaw protection
  • Steel/composite toecap (200J impact) — para 103a
  • Penetration-resistant midsole — protects against ground hazards (para 104b)
  • Anti-slip sole — reduces slip risk on uneven ground (para 104c)
  • Chainsaw protection on top of foot (EN 381-3) — para 103d
  • Inspect for wear and loose seams before use (para 105b)
  • No trainers, wellingtons or non-rated footwear on site
🔊 Section 7 — Hearing Protection (L25 para 113–114)
Note: Hearing protection is covered by the Control of Noise at Work Regulations 2005, not PPE Regs — BUT must be compatible with all other PPE worn (Reg 5, para 113)
Ear Defenders (Earmuffs)
  • Standard: EN 352 — SNR 28 dB minimum
  • Chainsaws: 100–115 dB(A) — mandatory protection
  • Hard plastic cups over ears, cushion-sealed (para 114b)
  • Helmet-mounted preferred for compatibility (Reg 5)
Earplugs
  • Fit into/cover ear canal — form a seal (para 114a)
  • Disposable — single use only, discard correctly
  • Acceptable as secondary protection alongside defenders
  • Must be compatible with helmet and visor (Reg 5)
🔗 Section 8 — Personal Fall Protection (L25 para 110–112)
ℹ All climbing equipment must be inspected before EVERY use. Pre-use check required by Regulation 7 (maintenance). LOLER 1998 also applies to lifting equipment used to raise persons.
Work Restraint System (para 111a)

Prevents user reaching zones where risk of fall exists. Does not arrest a fall — prevents access to fall zone.

Standard: EN 358 — work positioning lanyards
Work Positioning System (para 111b)

Supports operative in suspension while working. Both hands free. MUST have fall arrest back-up system.

Standards: EN 813 (sit harness) + EN 354 (lanyards)
Fall Arrest System (para 111e)

Arrests fall and limits force on body to max 6 kN. Energy absorbing lanyards or inertia reel devices.

Standards: EN 361 (full harness) + EN 355 (energy absorber)
Key Points for Fall Protection (L25 para 112)
Para 112a: Consider max descent height, load, secure anchor points, rope length and recovery system
Para 112b: Inspect equipment at regular intervals. LOLER requires 6-monthly examination by competent person
Para 112c: Webbing and rope deteriorates — inspect every metre for cuts, abrasion, stiffness and discolouration
⚠ Key rule: Maintain 2-point connection at ALL times — never unclip both lanyards simultaneously
📄 Section 9 — PPE Management Duties (L25 Regs 4–11)
Employer Duties
  • Reg 4: Provide suitable, correct-fitting PPE free of charge (para 37)
  • Reg 5: Ensure all PPE worn together is compatible
  • Reg 6: Assess PPE is suitable before issuing
  • Reg 7: Maintain, clean and replace PPE as needed
  • Reg 8: Provide appropriate storage for PPE
  • Reg 9: Provide information, instruction and training
Employee Duties
  • Reg 10: Use PPE properly as instructed and trained
  • Reg 10(4): Return PPE to storage after use
  • Reg 11: Report any loss or obvious defect immediately
  • Take reasonable care of PPE provided
  • Do not modify or damage PPE
  • Attend all PPE training provided
PPE Suitability (Reg 4(3))
  • Appropriate for the specific risk involved
  • Takes account of ergonomic needs and health of wearer
  • Capable of fitting the wearer correctly
  • Effective without increasing overall risk
  • Bears CE mark — Personal Protective Equipment Regs 2002 (para 44)
  • Employer cannot charge employees for PPE (para 37)
999
Fire / Police / Ambulance
0800 404 090
National Gas Emergency
105
Power Cut / Electricity
HSE Infoline
0300 003 1747
Health & Safety Executive
Arborite Tree Services Ltd  |  Based on HSE L25 Personal Protective Equipment at Work Regulations 1992 (Third Edition 2015)  |  Review annually

Common Factor Risk Assessments

Arborite Tree Services Ltd · Version 9 · 13.06.2025 · Due for review: 13.06.2026
Note: This is a controlled document except when printed. Release to persons not employed by Arborite Tree Services Ltd must first be authorised by a company Director.
Scope

As a company we complete assessments of the risks arising from all our operational activities. Each assessment details the controls and safe working procedures that need to be in place to ensure the health and safety of employees and others who may be affected by our work activities.

Risk assessments are completed for all work sites, this is company policy, and it is the responsibility of those individuals supervising works to ensure that this is carried out. All employees regardless of level of employment or status within the company, are actively encouraged to participate in the risk assessment process and will always be consulted.

Objectives and Process

The objectives of the generic risk assessments are to:

  1. Identify hazards (something with the potential to cause harm or damage).
  2. Identify those people likely to be affected by the hazard.
  3. Evaluate the level of risk (the likelihood of harm and severity of its consequences).

The process continues with the selection of controls that will:

  1. Eliminate the risk; or
  2. Reduce the risk to an acceptable or tolerable level.

Implementing all the controls and monitoring their effectiveness is essential. The aim is to establish a safe system of work with adequate controls in place to prevent injury or damage occurring.

Responsibilities
Responsibility Named Individual Position
Overall responsibility for HSEQ management and implementation Jon Challinor Managing Director
Competent advisors relating to HSEQ management Arb Matters Ltd External Advisors
Methodology

Generic risk assessments have been completed to show initially "worst case scenario" with no controls in place.

A completed assessment will show:

  1. The hazard.
  2. Risk, indicating the initial risk rating (likelihood × severity) without controls in place and how parties may be harmed.
  3. Who is at risk.
  4. Relevant good practice information to help support and promote suitable controls.
  5. The controls required for safe working.
  6. Controlled risk rating with specified control measures in place.

A hazard matrix has been used to quantify the level of risk in relation to the likelihood and severity from a hazard.

Risk Matrix
Risk Rating = L × S Very Unlikely (1) Unlikely (2) Likely (3) Very Likely (4) Certain (5)
Minimal Injury (1) 1 2 3 4 5
Minor Injury (2) 2 4 6 8 10
Moderate Injury (3) 3 6 9 12 15
Major Injury (4) 4 8 12 16 20
Fatality (5) 5 10 15 20 25
Severity Scale (S)
Minimal InjuryRequires no/minimal intervention or treatment, no lost time. Negligible environmental impact
Minor InjuryOr illness, requiring minor intervention. Minor environmental impact
Moderate InjuryRequires professional intervention, notifiable under RIDDOR, greater than 7 days lost time. Localised environmental impact
Major InjuryLeading to long term incapacity/disability. Serious environmental impact
Fatality(including multiple). Environmental disaster
Likelihood Scale (L)
Very UnlikelySo unlikely that probability is close to zero
UnlikelyUnlikely to occur, although conceivable
LikelyCould occur at some time
Very LikelyNot surprising, will occur several times
CertainOccurs repeatedly / event expected
Risk Level Tolerability Level Actions Required
High Intolerable Risk Work not started or immediately stopped
Medium Tolerable Risk Work may proceed, however adequate supervision must be in place combined with ongoing risk assessment review as the task progresses
Low Acceptable Risk No further preventative controls required. Monitor to ensure controls are maintained
Table of Amendments and Updates
Version Date Detail

601.06.2023Mowing risk assessment split into flail/swipe, pedestrian and ride on machines
Flail assessments merged
Compact loader and skid steer assessments merged
Mobile crane risk assessment added
701.06.2024Tree felling control measures updated to include dealing with trees affected by ADB
Rigging and dismantling controls updated to better reflect TG3
Proofread of good practices references to ensure validity
825.09.2024Document date change to align with other company assessments
913.06.2025Protective fencing risk assessment added
Reference to 7 days removed from severity scale
Assessment headers formatted
Delimbing and crosscutting risk assessment completely revised
Driving risk assessment risk column renumbered
Forestry Commission water guideline document updated

Due Date for Latest Revision13th June 2026
AuthorChris Cooper-Abbs, HSEQ Advisor
Approved ByJon Challinor, Managing Director

Driving↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Pre-driving vehicle checksFailure to ensure vehicle is roadworthy may lead to vehicle collisions3515Operator
Work Team
Public		HSG136: A guide to workplace transport safety
INDG 382: Managing work-related road safety
  • All exterior lights checked
  • Engine oil and water levels checked, including anti-freeze levels
  • Water levels in washer bottles checked
  • Tyres checked for condition and pressure
  • All windows and mirrors cleaned
  • Fuel level checked
155
Driving in adverse weather conditionsAdverse weather can increase likelihood of vehicle collisions and/or loss of vehicle control4520Operator
Work Team
Public
  • Local and national weather forecasts checked before setting off
  • If severe weather is forecast, journey should be delayed or cancelled
  • Vehicles to be maintained as part of ongoing company maintenance procedures
  • Manner of driving adjusted to account for the adverse weather conditions – reduce speed, increase distance from vehicles in front
155
Breakdown preparationFailure to prepare for breakdowns may put driver at risk3412Operator
Work Team
Public
  • Vehicle to carry tools for changing a wheel
  • Driver to have a torch for use in the dark
  • Driver to carry emergency telephone numbers in the event of a breakdown
  • Driver to always carry emergency kit including first-aid kit, Hi-visibility vest and fire extinguisher
144
Hitching trailer to vehicle1. Damage to vehicle from trailer
2. Back or muscle injury to operator
3. Slip or trip
4. Road Traffic Collision – trailer becomes detached, lights not correctly fitted
5. Uncontrolled movement of trailer/run away		4416Operator
Public		Transportation and Logistics Forum – Safe coupling and uncoupling guide
INDG199: Workplace transport safety
  • Where possible attach trailer to vehicle prior to any loading of trailer
  • Operators footwear to provide a good grip and ankle support
  • When multiple operators involved in the task, a single person should be designated as in control
  • Use of a trained banks person to aid in the reversing of vehicles
  • Preference should always be given to moving the vehicle to the trailer
  • Always ensure that all safety devices are engaged
  • Ensure the parking brake on the trailer has been applied prior to uncoupling
  • Ensure the ground is firm and level and will support any landing legs
  • Avoid leaving trailers on an adverse incline
  • Avoid leaving trailers where coupling may be difficult
  • Never place fingers in any ball joint locking mechanism
  • Do not attempt to lift trailers; changes in height to be completed via the trailers jockey wheel
  • To avoid jockey collapse, always carry out hitching and unhitching operations on level ground and ensure that the jockey is firmly in place before detaching from vehicles
  • Check all trailer connections are fully operational
  • Secure wheels with chocks if leaving a detached trailer on a gradient
  • Ensure all trailers are well maintained and in good order to prevent failure of safety features
  • Never attempt to manually assist the hitching process by kicking or pushing brakes, jockeys or other couplings
  • No person should stand on/in a trailer while coupling and decoupling
144
Emergency Contingencies↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Staff or other personnel on site suffer injury, including infection with communicable disease1. Fatality
2. Ill health
3. Worsening of injury
4. Worsening of condition
5. Complications
6. Viral infection
7. Psychological trauma		4520Operator
Work Team
Client		FISA Safety Guide 802 – Emergency Planning
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
AA TG1: Tree climbing and aerial rescue
FISA 806 – Welfare
  • Appropriate First Aid Kit on site
  • Climbers carry personal First Aid Kit
  • All staff trained and certificated in First Aid
  • At least one other staff member trained, equipped and certificated in Aerial Rescue
  • All staff know site location and means of contacting emergency services
  • All staff trained to complete Accident Book/Incident Report Form
  • Operator awareness, guidance, information and instruction
  • Items of concern carefully handled with protective gloves and disposed of correctly
  • Treat all blood or body fluid as infected and take universal precautions – use of gloves, wash hands thoroughly
  • Bag items carefully and seal bag
  • Hand cleansing products available to operators
  • Health monitoring and surveillance
  • Always carry out good hygiene practices e.g. washing and covering cuts with a sterile dressing, washing hands after work and prior to eating and drinking
155
Fire/explosion1. Burns
2. Blast injuries		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA Safety Guide 301 – Using petrol driven chainsaws
302 – Basic chainsaw felling and manual takedown
AA TG2: Safe use of tools in the tree
  • Shaded fuelling sites selected when practicable
  • Safe distance applied from any ignition source
  • Correct marked and sealed containers or bunded storage
  • Fuel caps replaced securely
  • Spill provision provided along with operator training
  • Use of spouts, funnels and pumps to be utilised as appropriate
  • PPE to be worn in accordance with up to date company COSHH assessment (Petrol, grease, oil)
  • Fire extinguisher serviced and available
144
Emergency call-out work: night-time work, poor weather conditionsCut/crush/impact injury due to poor visibility, unstable trees or structures, other people/vehicles on site4416Operator
Work team
Public
Client		FISA Safety Guide 301 – Using petrol driven chainsaws, 302 – Basic chainsaw felling and manual takedown, 303 – Snedding, 304 – Chainsaw cross cutting and manual stacking, 306 – Chainsaw clearance of windblow, 307 – Chainsaw felling of large trees, 310 – Use of winches in directional felling and takedown, 501 – Tractor units in tree work
HSE INDG317 Chainsaws at work
HSE HSG47 Avoiding danger from underground services
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Co-ordination with emergency services, local authorities, utility company etc.
  • Portable lighting on site
  • Unauthorised personnel excluded from work area
  • PPE used in accordance with good practice and must include hi-visibility clothing
  • Amber warning beacons fitted to vehicles and used during call out/night time work
144
Operations Involving Young Persons↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Reduced perception of existing or potential risks due to lack of experience and maturity; Exposure to potentially hazardous environments; Task beyond Young Person's psychological or physical capacity; Fire/emergency within workplace/worksite; Exposure to excessive noise, vibration, heat or coldIncreased risk of injury from worksite hazards; machinery, equipment and falling objects
Risk of accidents/injury from: slips, trips or falls; falling objects; being struck or crushed by site plant; COSHH; exposure to radiation; lone working
Increased risk of injury due to task being too complicated or lifting significant loads
Accident, fatality or injury; Workplace injury		4520Young PersonINDG 364 HSE Guidance – Young Persons at Work
  • Assessment of experience and maturity of Young Person at Company induction stage
  • Nomination of a designated and fully competent Supervisor/Line Manager
  • Provision of additional supervision, training and mentoring by a nominated colleague via a buddy system where appropriate
  • To ensure that all machinery and equipment is safe to use and in good working order
  • Provision of clear instruction and direction in relation to Young Person's designated tasks
  • Full briefing of and adherence to controls set down within Company's Generic and Site-Specific risk assessment and/or method statements
  • Young Person will not be allowed to lone work or operate high risk machinery and equipment
  • Young Person will not work with a chainsaw or any other high-risk equipment or at height
  • Young Person will not be involved with the lifting or handling of significant loads
  • Appropriate Company awareness training in relation to fire/emergency procedures
  • Supervisor/Line manager to oversee evacuation of Young Person from worksite hazard
  • Controls in place to ensure that Young Person is not exposed to excessive noise, vibration, heat or cold through effective and appropriate induction and ongoing supervision and mentoring by a designated Supervisor/Line Manager
  • Continual assessment of Young Person's role and tasking within the Company
  • Provision of appropriate PPE
155
Site Considerations↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Theft of work equipment from works vehicles. Theft of works vehicles, including trailed loads1. Serious injury/fatality arising from untrained use/abuse/misuse of stolen work equipment
2. Severe injury from contact with stolen vehicle moving at speed		4520Operator
Work Team
Public
Client
Emergency Services Personnel
  • Awareness training for all operators
  • Comply with all in-company security instructions
  • Do not leave machinery running when unattended
  • Do not leave ignition keys in unattended vehicles or idle plant such as brushwood chippers or stump grinders
  • Internal procedures designed to ensure security of work equipment on site
155
Weather: Extremes of temperature; wet, windy, electrical storm1. Poor visibility
2. Loss of grip/slipping
3. Physiological stress
4. Hypothermia
5. Electrocution
6. Dehydration
7. Sunburn
8. Sunstroke		4416Work TeamFISA Safety Guide 302 – Basic chainsaw felling and manual takedown
AFAG 802 – Emergency planning
C&G NPTC/Lantra Awards L2/3 Competencies
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
AA TG1: Tree climbing and aerial rescue
FISA 806 - Welfare
  • Change task
  • Stop work when hazard affects ability to carry out work in accordance with industry good practice
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Thermal blanket/first aid kit available
  • If de-hydration symptoms arise stop work and rest in a cool place. If symptoms persist seek medical advice from nearest A&E
  • Ensure adequate head protection is worn. Drink adequate liquids at regular intervals. If Sun Stroke symptoms arise rest in a cool place. If symptoms persist seek medical advice from nearest A&E
  • All staff are provided access to sun protection cream. Ensure it is applied according to instructions. Always wear uniform as issued. Reduce exposure by rotating work between sun and shade areas
  • Ensure good grip is achievable, do not work on slopes if conditions are unsafe
  • Facilities provided for operators to dry work clothing and applicable equipment
144
Terrain1. Slipping/tripping4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA Safety Guide 302, 307, 802
AA TG1
  • Prepare site – walk site prior to starting work to identify significant hazards
  • Change task; Change access
  • Appropriate footwear for staff/tyres for vehicles
144
Steep slopes or edge working1. Crush injuries from overturned machinery
2. Operator thrown from machinery during use
3. Material rolling		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		HSG144 The safe use of vehicles on construction sites
INDG185 Using tractors safely
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated to operate on steep slopes
  • Do not use machinery on slopes/gradients over the manufacturers recommended limits
  • Ensure where fitted roll over protection system is in place
  • Where fitted seat belts are always to be worn
  • Using machinery across the face of a slope to be avoided unless machinery is specifically designed for this purpose
  • Brake operation checked frequently and brakes are regularly serviced as required
  • Movement on slopes is slow and gradual, up and down movement
  • Starting, stopping or turning of machinery is avoided on a slope, if the tyres/tracks lose traction cutting systems where fitted should be disengaged and the operator should proceed slowly down the slope
  • Care is taken to watch for hidden holes or soft ground following heavy rain and the speed is reduced
  • Passengers are never carried on machinery
  • Edge protection used to demarcate the edge
  • Machine has adequate purchase on working surface to prevent slippage
  • Care to be taken during slew operations to avoid overbalancing and inertia
  • Down slope is cordoned off to prevent access
  • Bunding or alternative restraints used where material can roll and/or fall down slopes
144
Direct contact – body parts touch or in very close proximity to electrical conductors; Indirect contact; Electrical equipment overloads or damage; Individuals approach live fallen or broken lines or cables; Induced current; Exposure to arching; Electricity released causes fire1. Electrocution, burns, organ and tissue damage, nerve damage
2. Loss of equipment control
3. Light damage to eyes
4. Fire		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		GS6 – Avoiding danger from overhead power lines
HSG (47) – Avoiding danger from underground services
FISA 804 – Electricity at Work: Forestry
G55 Engineering Recommendation Safe Tree Working in Proximity to Overhead Electric Lines
Engineering Technical Report 136 Vegetation Management Near Electricity Equipment
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Site inspections and risk assessment shall include identification and categorisation of electric conductors
  • Works to be carried out in compliance with GS6 – HSG47 – G55
  • Contact with power or electrical distribution company if proximity zone is breached (OHL) Zone 1 – 2 x tree length of trees to be felled; Zone 2 – including arboricultural operations, machine operations within 9m lines on wooden poles, 15m on metal towers
  • Underground cables shall be CAT scanning by trained operator
  • Schematics referenced to determine the presence of UG services – services if located shall be marked
  • Assessment of the requirement to de-energise conductors made by REC representative
  • Operator training in electrical hazards – issue of AFAG 804
  • Operators are to be familiar with onsite emergency procedures including contact with OHPL and UG services, REC emergency contact details available
  • Erection of goalposts as defined within GS6 where crossing beneath OHPLs is required
  • Machinery displays maximum travelling height
  • Daily work site briefings shall be carried out with staff to remind them of the hazards/risks and controls to be implemented
  • Team leader to ensure a site-specific risk assessment is completed, including operator briefing
  • Operators shall not approach nor touch broken lines
  • As part of SSRA completion operators and the team leader must identify likely combustible materials – inform REC
  • Timber not stacked directly beneath OHPL; Woodchip piles spread, not stockpiled
  • Timber lengths/branches processed in small enough lengths to prevent breach of the vicinity zone if picked up
  • Site access routes always walked first prior to any equipment or machinery entering site – hazards identified are to be dealt with as part of SSRA completion
248
Other utility wayleaves (e.g. Telephone lines, fibre optic, gas mains, water or sewage pipes)1. Rupturing gas/water/telecomm supplies
2. Explosions
3. Loss of data		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		AA Technical Guide One: Tree climbing and aerial rescue
HSE HSG47 Avoiding danger from underground services
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
  • Establish existence/position of wayleaves
  • CAT/GENNY used by trained operator
  • Avoid excavation or excavate only using hand tools
  • Avoid significant ground impact from falling timber
  • Avoid contact with overhead telecomm lines
144
Buildings or other obstructionsTrees/parts of trees in contact with building or obstruction4312Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA Safety Guide 302, 307, 310
AA Technical Guide One
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
  • Work planning
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Buildings evacuated as necessary
133
Working near roads/footpaths/public areas1. Trees/parts of trees hitting passing traffic/pedestrians
2. Workers struck by passing traffic		4520Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		AA TG1
Department for Transport ACOP "Safety at street works and road works" to support NRSWA
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Adequate staff on site to maintain traffic/pedestrian control
  • Use of "Hi-viz" clothing/PPE
  • Public excluded from work area by erecting signs, barriers and/or tape on all reasonably foreseeable approaches
  • Tools are not unattended
  • Worksite is designated as a no smoking area
  • Vehicles parked safely and courteously – access routes are not blocked
  • Sites are to be left in a clean and tidy state at the end of each working day
155
Working in public open spaces1. Potential for mis-understanding of behaviour around members of the public
2. Injury to members of the public from debris and unattended machinery
3. Potential to adversely influence the development of children, vulnerable adults, young adults
4. Injury from vehicle movement
5. Injury caused by parking in a position which obscures lines of sight
6. Legitimate photography may cause alarm especially if children are in the park		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
Client
  • Tools and machinery must not be left unattended
  • Over-familiarity with members of the public is open to misinterpretation. If the site is visited by an interested passer-by, the team leader will politely answer questions with a view to the person moving on
  • Staff must not smoke on the worksite
  • Vehicles must not be driven within the public open space without strict supervision
  • A banksman must walk in front of the vehicle from entering the park to its arrival at the worksite
  • Beacons must be used, and the speed must be kept to a walking pace
  • Vehicles must not be parked in a manner preventing access for public service vehicles, e.g. ambulance, fire tenders, etc.
  • Welfare arrangements must be planned prior to work commencing on site
  • All vehicles, plant and equipment must be contained well within the site boundary marked with barrier tape
  • Radios must not be used in a public open space as they might cause a disturbance
  • Staff must be prepared to politely explain the purpose of photography if anyone enquires
  • Staff must strictly observe the standard company policy on the use of harsh or offensive language
  • The site must be left in a safe condition with all arising waste material cleared completely even if the team are to return the following day
133
Working in sensitive/emotionally charged locality1. Offending or alienating the general public
2. Work team becoming the victim of verbal or physical abuse		5315Operator
Work Team
Public
Client
Local Special Interest Group
Anti-social element of local community
  • Refer situation to supervisor/team leader/manager/client
  • Seek to calm the tensions
  • Bad or offensive language must not be used by company staff under any circumstances
  • Hours/timings of work considered as part of the work planning process
133
Working on construction sites1. Injury caused by other contractors also on site
2. Slips, trips and falls resulting from uneven and unmade surfaces or site debris
3. Falls or vehicles driving into unmarked excavations		4416Operator
Work Team
Client		FISA 808 – CDM in Relation to Forestry
  • Site induction when provided attended by all operators
  • All site rules must be followed
  • The site-specific risk assessment must consider all site hazards including the potential for slips, trips and falls
  • Staff must ensure that all operations, tools, equipment, vehicles and debris remain within the designated worksite boundary
  • PPE must accord with the specific site rules
  • Walk a site prior to tracking or movement of plant and equipment
144
AsbestosRisk of exposure to harmful asbestos fibres (considered low risk in this format, i.e. asbestos cement)4416Operatives
Work Team
Possibly Clients/Residents		HSE Managing asbestos in buildings (a brief guide)
Asbestos cement (information sheet)
  • Operators trained in the identification of asbestos within the format of asbestos cement
  • Where the presence of asbestos is suspected and/or discovered, work is to be stopped immediately and senior management informed
144
Other work activities going on near to work area1. Distraction from other on-site activities or unplanned events4312Operator
Work Team
Public
  • Establish effective method of communication with all operators before starting task
  • Communication with other parties on site
  • Stop all activities until area is safe/clear
133
Mud on road or tracks1. Road traffic accident4416Operator
Work Team
Public		HSG144 The safe use of vehicles on construction sites
  • Where possible operations to be scheduled in dry conditions
  • Avoid highway use where contamination is likely (alternative off road route)
  • Clean machinery off before entering highway by using wash down areas
  • Road signage to be erected warning road users of "Mud on Road"
  • Periodic cleaning of the highway utilising road brush or sweeper, particularly during the duration of the work, once work has been completed and at the end of each working day
  • Operators must only enter the highway when safe to do so and whilst wearing compliant hi-visibility PPE
144
Working adjacent to, on or over water1. Accidental immersion in water leading to drowning
2. Hypothermia
3. Health risk from contact with aquatic environments – Weils disease, Polio, Hep A, Tetanus, toxic-cyano bacteria, blue-green algae, pollution
4. Impact with submerged objects
5. Impact with floating or submerged debris
6. Manual handling of wet materials
7. Trip and slip hazards on the bank resulting in fall into water
8. Entrapment between bank and working platform
9. Fall from working platform
10. Exposure from cold water
11. Confrontation from other waterway users		4520Operator
Work Team
Public		FISA 806 - Welfare
  • No lone working – work only allowed in pairs when working around or on water
  • People always need to be in sight of each other
  • Communication devices are necessary, and they must be waterproof, suitable to the area of operation and tested before work commences
  • An emergency plan, including provision for rescue and contact, if necessary, with the emergency services established
  • Adequate provision must be made for first aid
  • Lifebuoys, meeting an approved standard with a suitable buoyant lifeline of adequate length attached, should be available within around 20m of any working position where a person could fall into the water
  • Safety boat should always be available
  • Any operator working near water must be able to swim unaided for at least 25m
  • Hand cleansing materials are to be provided
  • Adequate training of operators to include cold water first aid and water rescue
  • Facilities for warmth and shelter may be required
  • Staff and visitors shall endeavour to not come into contact with water in so far as is reasonably practicable
  • It should be ensured that all staff are within date for tetanus vaccination
  • Where practicable to do so, operators shall wear buoyancy jackets when working in the proximity of waterways. It is accepted however that the wearing of a life jacket could present a specific hazard to operators of arboricultural machinery
  • Working platforms are either tethered to the bank, so no risk exists of operators falling between the two surfaces, or the use of mooring buoys are deployed
  • If blue-green algae or pollution is present in the worksite postpone the task
  • Cover all wounds with a waterproof dressing
  • Wash hands before touching eyes, face, mouth, and eating, drinking or smoking
  • Work will NOT take place in water in which sewage is known to be present
  • Store materials away from walkways and banks
  • Do not work from wet or slippery banks next to deep or fast flowing water
  • Whenever reasonably practicable fixed edge protection must be provided to prevent people falling into water
  • Where edge barriers are not reasonably practicable at exposed edges, appropriate warning signs and/or edge markings should be displayed – An alternative is the use of a full body harness attached by lanyard to a suitable anchorage point or proprietary fall prevention anchorage system
  • All operators PPE to include as a minimum boots with good grip and ankle support
  • Work to be avoided where a conflict of interest may exist with other waterway users
  • Seek to discuss multi occupancy of waterways with other users to co-ordinate work and leisure activities timings
155
Setting up and removing temporary traffic management1. Operators struck by moving vehicles – major injury, fatality
2. Operators struck by road or vehicle debris – major injury, fatality
3. Musculoskeletal injuries from moving and handling traffic management equipment		4520Operator
Work Team
Public		C!S53 reducing risk in temporary traffic management operations
  • Traffic management workers require good standards of physical fitness, eyesight and hearing. Their health must be assessed before they are assigned to traffic management activities. Health assessments should ensure that workers: (a) have full, unrestricted, use of neck, trunk and legs; (b) have at least 6/12 distance vision when wearing glasses or contact lenses; (c) have good hearing; (d) are suitable for this work if they suffer from specific conditions, e.g. vertigo and balance disorders, psychotic illnesses, diabetes, cardiovascular and gastrointestinal conditions, and sleeping disorders; (e) are not taking inappropriate medication, illegal drugs, or drinking excessive amounts of alcohol
  • Operators shall have received adequate training and hold relevant certification. Operators: C&G Unit 2 – Signing, Lighting and Guarding certificate. Supervisors: C&G Unit 10 – Signing, Lighting and Guarding certificate
  • Operators must have access to a supervisor when setting out traffic management
  • Operators are to have received manual handling training and manual handling risk assessments in place
  • Transport of traffic management equipment by hand shall be minimised by using vehicles or other equipment such as sack trucks or trolleys
  • The worksite must be signed in accordance with the guidance in the "Red Book" – Safety at Street Works and Road Works
  • All operators must wear high visibility outerwear suitable for the classification of the road
  • Operators must remain inside the safe system of work at all times and must not step onto the live highway
  • Operatives should only work in live lane(s) once advance signs are in place to warn road users of their presence. Where possible, operatives should face the oncoming traffic, or work with a lookout
  • Advance signing is important for alerting drivers to the presence of workers in the carriageway on foot. Advance signs must be placed so that all road users can see them clearly before workers set foot on the carriageway
  • Positioning of works vehicles should support operatives and minimise the need to cross the carriageway. They should be positioned to prevent funnelling of drivers toward the work area or into the path of oncoming traffic
  • The sequence of work for removing the equipment must be clear and detailed
  • Operators must have maximum protection and drivers given sufficient warning of the presence of workers in the road at all times until the equipment is removed
  • Before removing the traffic management layout, the area must be clear of workers, vehicles, equipment or debris, and any excavations must be filled or covered
  • All works vehicles are to be in good condition, driven by qualified drivers and comply with the requirements of Chapter 8 of the Traffic Signs Manual Part 2: Operations, section 5 and Safety at Street Works and Road Works, Part 3. Reflective signs and panels are to be clean and all lights and beacons should be clean and in working order
  • No lone working is allowed
155
Environmental Considerations↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Mineral oil/herbicides/pesticides in or near watercourses – Tree work adjacent to watercourses1. Pollution threat to aquatic life forms, be they plant or animal
2. Sawdust, chippings, brushwood, timber causing blockage to watercourse		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		BS 3998: Recommendations for Tree Work
Forest Research: Managing forest operations to protect the water environment
FISA Safety Guide 301 – Using petrol driven chainsaws
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Refuelling of vehicles, plant and equipment to be carried out away from watercourses
  • No mixing and decanting of pesticides and herbicides or other chemicals near a watercourse
  • Gain Environment Agency licence for herbicide application within 20m of a watercourse
  • Ensure watercourses and banks are kept clear of arisings from tree work
  • Machinery where possible/applicable filled with bio-degradable oils
144
Proposed work adjacent to protected species/habitat1. Damage to protected species or habitat
2. Damage to badger setts and so community of badgers
3. Disturbance to nesting or roosting places
4. Disturbance to habitat		4416Operator
Work Team		English Nature "Bat mitigation guidelines"
Arboricultural Association Guidance Note 1 "Trees and Bats" Conservation Habitat Regulations
  • Experienced surveyor to carry out visual check before works commence. If protected species present either: delay works until animal moves off, of its own accord (e.g. otter, snake etc.); or delay works until a non-critical season – out of nesting, breeding or migration season; or change scope of works to avoid disturbance of habitat; or obtain relevant licence from English Nature (badger sett, bats, crayfish, watervoles) as appropriate for the species or habitat
  • Groundworks within 50m of an active badger sett may require a licence from English Nature
144
Excessive noise or noise at inappropriate times. Excessive dust1. Loss of enjoyment of property through noise or dust4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client
  • Only use correctly maintained and silenced vehicles, plant and equipment
  • Unless emergency work, limit working hours to 8:00 to 18:00 Monday to Friday
  • Site chipping equipment to avoid wind blowing debris onto private property
144
Biosecurity: Transmission of harmful organisms1. Spread of pests or diseases from site to site
2. Entering land where a risk of spreading pests exists
3. Contamination from run off/arisings entering watercourses
4. Damage to existing habitat		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		Forestry Commission England "Keep it clean"
Forestry Commission England – Biosecurity Guidance
FISA 806 – Welfare
  • All sites: Ensure operators are aware of biosecurity issues, and principles of good practice to minimise the spread of pests and diseases
  • Planning of operations should include consultation with landowners to identify if any restrictions are currently in place, or known biosecurity risks exist
  • Operators to report any concerns, or where unknown problems are found
  • When leaving sites remove plant material and soil from boots
  • Where possible clean soil and plant material from vehicles and equipment before leaving the site and visiting other sites
  • Only move arisings from sites if it is visually free from pests and diseases
  • Implement measures to avoid arisings entering watercourses
  • Keep vehicular access to a minimum: do not enter areas unnecessarily and, where practicable, keep to established hard tracks
  • All operators to observe any signs, and controls on site implemented to control biosecurity risks
  • Clean machinery regularly to avoid spreading material to new areas – Any cleaning down or disinfection activity must take place at least 10m from any watercourse
  • High risk sites: Clean and, where relevant, disinfect footwear; Clean and, where relevant, disinfect tools, particularly cutting equipment after each time they are used and before moving on to the next plant or tree; If vehicles have entered an area where a damaging tree pest is known or suspected to be present, and have been taken off hard roads, ensure that the tyres and wheel arches are adequately cleaned and disinfected well away from drains and water courses and before leaving the site
144
Machinery and Vehicle Movements on Site↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Machinery movement around site1. The moving machine – Crushing and laceration due to sharp objects
2. Collision with people or other vehicles/machinery due to lack of observation
3. Surface damage		4520Operator
Work Team		HSG144 The safe use of vehicles on construction sites
INDG199: Workplace transport safety
HSG136: A guide to workplace transport safety
  • Under no circumstances can a passenger be carried in or on machinery unless a seat is fitted for that purpose
  • Operator must be aware of hazards on site prior to driving and briefed clearly as to any route he/she is required to follow
  • Third parties must be kept at a safe distance from moving machinery using an exclusion zone, warning signs and/or verbal warnings
  • Operators must drive at a speed suitable for the machine, terrain, conditions, task and load
  • Check all-round observation before moving off and always be aware of others in or near work area
  • Use of high visibility vest or jacket (PPE)
  • Where fitted operator to be observant and aware of counterweight overhang
  • Minimise track skid when turning on sensitive surfaces
  • Utilise banks person where the site is restricted, open to the public or busy
  • Exercise caution when operating machinery in confined spaces
155
Machinery reversing1. Crush and/or impact injuries4520Operator
Work Team		HSG144 The safe use of vehicles on construction sites
INDG199: Workplace transport safety
HSG136: A guide to workplace transport safety
  • Implement one-way systems around site and in loading and unloading areas
  • Provide designated turning areas
  • Reduce the number of vehicle movements as far as possible
  • Instruct drivers not to reverse, unless necessary
  • Ensure vehicle reversing areas: allow adequate space for vehicles to manoeuvre safely; exclude pedestrians; and are clearly signed to have physical stops or buffers to warn drivers that they have reached the limit of the safe reversing area
  • Where reasonably practicable fit CCTV, convex mirrors, Fresnel lens etc. to overcome restrictions to visibility from the driver's seat, particularly at the sides and rear of vehicles
  • Where reasonably practicable fit radar proximity devices to vehicles to indicate to drivers when there are objects near the vehicle
  • Ensure everyone on site understands site rules on vehicle safety
  • Drivers and signallers need to be in constant communication during reversing operations
  • Signallers should not be put at risk from vehicle movements, e.g. by standing directly behind reversing vehicles
  • Ensure reversing warning lights and alarms are in good working order and instruct workers to keep clear of moving vehicles
  • Ensure only trained, authorised and where applicable certificated individuals undertake signaller tasks
155
Works vehicles1. Personal injury from contact with sharp edges (doors, lockers, tailgates, etc.)
2. Jump/fall from load bed, trailer, cab, etc.
3. Crush injuries from tipper bed, trailer, unexpected movement, etc.		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA Safety Guide 501 – Tractor units in tree work
INDG413: Preventing falls from vehicles
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • All guards and warnings in place
  • Regular vehicle maintenance and servicing
  • Loading and securing of load in accordance with Department for Transport guidelines
  • Appropriate PPE worn, such as safety helmet, safety boots and gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1)
  • Routine preventative maintenance in accordance with internal procedures and manufacturers' recommendations
  • Driver/operator does not jump down from cab/load bed etc.
  • Driver/operator to use handholds/steps as provided
144
Parking1. Collision with other vehicles
2. Injury due to unexpected movement of machinery
3. Unauthorised use		4520Operator
Work Team
Public		HSG144
INDG199
HSG136
  • Operator to park safely and in such a way that he/she can get out of the vehicle safely and that machine does not present significant risks to third parties
  • Attachments when parked made safe as per manufacturers guidance
  • While parked, parking brake must be applied, engine off, key removed
  • If left unattended vehicle must if possible be locked with ignition key removed
155
Machinery Maintenance and General Considerations↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Machine selection1. Machine failure or breakage
2. Overturn
3. Bogging down
4. Incompatible implements
5. Operators struck by materials
6. Poor visibility		3412Operator
Work Team
Public		CPCS Qualifications
HSG144 The safe use of vehicles on construction sites
  • Machine selection to consider: Stability and flotation (track length/width); Traction (grouser height/frequency); Ground clearance (raised undercarriage); Machine guarding to prevent damage to pipe work/components during the operation; Poor light conditions – adequate lighting fitted
  • Attachments to be compatible with any boom mountings
  • Operator Protective Structures (OPS), Falling Object Protective Structures (FOPS), Rollover Protective Structures (ROPS) requirements met
144
Operating equipment with which an individual is unfamiliar1. Injury due to incorrect use or handling of equipment
2. Delay in contacting emergency services in the event of an accident and/or incident
3. Machine damage		4520Operator
Work Team		CPCS Qualifications
INDG185 Using tractors safely
  • Operator to familiarise themselves with new or different equipment prior to its use
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Dealer/hirer training provided at handover
  • Tasks to be initially undertaken in safer low risk environments, prior to moving to higher risk and possibly complex environments
  • Utilise "Safe Stop" procedures when examining plant and machinery
  • Refer to manufacturer's instruction manual and/or specific risk assessment
155
Vibration1. Vibration-induced White Finger (VWF)
2. Hand Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS)		4416OperatorINDG175 Control the Risks from Hand-Arm Vibration – Guidance for Employers on Control of Vibration at Work Regulations
  • Select appropriate equipment to task, operator and environment
  • Monitor and carefully regulate exposure times against published data
  • Correct maintenance for A/V or dampening systems
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Health surveillance/HAVS monitoring
144
Noise1. Noise Induced Hearing Loss/Deafness4416Operator
Work Team
Public		INDG362 Noise at Work
INDG363 Protect Your Hearing or Lose It
  • Correct machine maintenance (exhaust baffles)
  • Hearing protectors supplied and to be used (as identified via specific workplace noise assessments)
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Health surveillance/hearing checks undertaken on employees
144
Exhaust fumes1. Nausea
2. Dizziness
3. Loss of concentration		4416Operator
Work Team
  • Machinery must not be run indoors to avoid fume build up
  • Maintenance regime undertaken in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Machines must not be operated in a confined or enclosed area, as operation in such an area may result in the accumulation of toxic fumes to a hazardous level
144
Poor handling1. Muscular strain/long-term injury4416OperatorINDG143 Getting to Grips with Manual Handling
  • Avoid manual handling where possible
  • All operators trained and where appropriate certificated
  • Follow safe manual handling procedures throughout the task
  • No individual may put themselves at risk by carrying out unsafe procedures
  • Use of manual handling aids and equipment as necessary
144
Fuelling, greasing and oil replenishment1. Fire/explosion
2. Burns/blast injuries		4416Operator
Work Team
Client
Public		HSG144
INDG370 Fire and explosion, how safe is your workplace
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Shaded fuelling site selected
  • Safe distance applied from any ignition source
  • Correct marked and sealed containers or bunded storage
  • Fuel caps replaced securely
  • Spill provision provided along with operator training given in the use of such systems
  • Spouts, funnels and pumps to be utilised as appropriate
  • PPE to be worn in accordance with up to date company COSHH assessment (Petrol, grease, oil)
  • When re-fuelling the manufacturer's instructions and any instructions on starting plant/equipment/machinery must be followed
  • Always ensure that no members of the public or other staff are in the area when refuelling
  • Staff use correct mix, cans and jugs. Do not over fill with fuel
  • Exhaust/engine guards to be in place
  • Staff are aware of hot exhaust and engine. Machinery allowed to cool prior to fuelling, greasing and oil replenishment
  • The fuel cap is NEVER removed whilst the engine is running
  • If fuel is spilled on clothing, clothing is changed immediately
  • Fire extinguisher serviced and available
  • Staff always move at least 3m from the fuelling point before restarting machinery
144
Mounting and dismounting – working at height1. Falls from machines or equipment4416OperatorINDG185 Using tractors safely
INDG413 Preventing falls from vehicles
  • Operators must follow safe procedure, normally entering and exiting cabs from left hand side using the steps and hand holds provided, facing the vehicle and not jumping or rushing
  • Clean footwear of mud before entering vehicle cab
  • Keep cab floor clear of any tools, equipment, debris removed from steps
  • Avoid wearing loose clothing
144
Pre-use checks, equipment, and routine maintenance1. Injury through entrapment, entanglement, hot surfaces, contamination, cuts, crush
2. Fall from height – machine
3. Dermatitis/skin disorders
4. Debris in eyes
5. Injury through accidental starting of machinery
6. Entanglement – Limb/digit severance		4416Operator
Work Team		HSG144 the safe use of vehicles on construction sites
INDG185 Using tractors safely
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Ensure safe stop procedures are in place
  • Allow components to become stationary and cool down
  • No unauthorised modifications to equipment or machinery to be undertaken
  • Any repairs, adjustments etc. must only be carried out by a competent person
  • Do not wear loose clothing and ensure long hair is tied back
  • Wear safety helmet to EN397 or EN12492 when working below machine parts
  • Wear safety footwear with good grip
  • Wear gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1), considering grip, cut resistant, absorption
  • Manufacturers handbook to be made available and consulted
  • No individual to climb upon the machinery structure
  • Use of designated access steps, ladder and gantries only when access required
  • Adequate access equipment (step ladder) to be provided
  • Work is only carried out in good light and weather conditions
144
Changing and Using Machinery Attachments↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Changing of attachments – general1. Unexpected movement of equipment causing crush or entrapment
2. Musculoskeletal injury from lifting or adjusting the attachment
3. Contamination from grease
4. Failure of attachment through incorrect fitting		4416Operator
Work Team		Construction Plant Hire Association (Strategic Form Plant Safety Group): Safe use of Quickhitches
INDG185 Using tractors safely
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Ensure machine and attachment manufacturers handbook is available
  • Ensure hitch systems are compatible
  • Attachments to be used must be authorised by the manufacturer for the machine type
  • Ensure when used operator and third-party communication is clear and hand signals established
  • Appropriate PPE to include: safety footwear, non-snag outer clothing, gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1)
  • No work to take place beneath unsupported components
  • Ensure attachment and machine are stationary and stable (attachment fitting only on level ground)
  • Operational controls to be operated only from manufacturers designated working positions e.g. on operators' seat, as opposed to leaning inside cab
  • Operator to ensure attachment is correctly attached before moving away ensuring where fitted all pins have lynch pins, pick up hooks correctly engaged, use of "shake, rattle and roll" where appropriate
  • Maintenance conducted on attachments as per manufacturers guidance by competent and authorised individuals
144
Changing of attachments – connection of hydraulics & electrics1. High pressure hydraulic injection
2. Struck by coupling
3. Personal contamination
4. Burns from electric connection
5. High pressure air injection		4416Operator
Work Team		INDG185 Using tractors safely
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Ensure machine and attachment manufacturers handbook is available
  • Appropriate PPE to include: safety footwear, non-snag outer clothing, gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1)
  • Ensure hydraulic couplings are compatible, undamaged and clean prior to connection
  • Operator to ensure system is not pressurised
  • De-pressurise couplings safely using correct procedures – check rams are not under load, only depress coupling valve lock away from operator avoiding contamination (PPE to be worn)
  • Do not check pressurised systems for leaks by hand
  • Ensure all operators are clear prior to pressurising a system
  • Ensure electrical couplings are compatible, undamaged and clean prior to connection
144
Power take-offs and power take-off drive shafts1. Entanglement
2. Damage to drive shaft, attachment and power source		4520OperatorAS24 Power take-offs and power take-off drive shafts
  • Vehicle PTO guarded with a shield covering the top and both sides
  • Use of a full-length PTO drive shaft guard
  • Guards to be CE marked when supplied with a PTO drive shaft
  • Separately purchased guards must conform to BS EN ISO 5674: 2006
  • Correct fitting of guards in accordance with both manufacturers' guidance and HSE AS24
  • Bystanders warned by sounding the horn prior to engaging a PTO drive
  • No loose or damaged clothing to be worn, long hair to be tied back
  • Never try to clear blockages from a PTO drive machine whilst the shaft is moving
  • Ensure the correct drive shaft and engine speed are used
  • Ensure all components, including the drive shaft and guards are compatible
  • Unless external controls are fitted, 3-point linkage, attachment lifting and lowering and PTO engagement must only be carried out from inside the vehicle cab
  • Operators trained, authorised and where applicable certificated in PTO use
155
Use of attachments: Brush rake, root crop bucket, timber/selector grab etc.1. Injury through manual efforts to move attachment
2. Failure of attachment
3. Ejected material
4. Struck by material
5. Uncontrolled or unexpected movement of the attachment		4416OperatorCITB Health and Safety advice for Plant Operators
  • Operators trained, authorised and where applicable certificated in attachment and base unit use
  • Ensure the make and model of attachment is compatible with the base machine
  • Load charts to be available to, and adhered to by operators
  • Fitting and removal of the attachment completed only be authorised and competent operators
  • Attachment to be CE marked
  • Attachment subject to thorough examination intervals as defined under LOLER and PUWER
  • Attachment to be placed upon ground after use
  • Base unit machine to be fitted with ROPS/FOPS
  • Attachment fitted in accordance with manufacturer's instructions
  • No modifications or alterations to take place to the attachment unless authorised by the manufacturer
  • Maintenance completed by competent operators only
  • When grabbed material extends beyond the width of the base unit cab, safe working distances of at least 2 x longest product length must be maintained
  • Operator must not float or suspend loads over persons, plant or structures
  • Operator to complete pre-use check of attachment including hydraulic connections
  • Do not attempt to lift or manoeuvre attachment manually
  • An exclusion zone to prevent third parties entering the work area is to be established and maintained – the use of tape/barriers/banksman must be used in areas of public activity
  • Work shall not be carried out where any part of the machine or its load can come within 15m of an OHPL without first consulting the network operator
144
Use of trailed attachments: Auto-reset ripper, stone crusher, grade blade, soil grader, ripper hook etc.1. Injury through manual efforts to move attachment
2. Failure of attachment
3. Ejected material
4. Struck by material
5. Uncontrolled or unexpected movement of the attachment		4416OperatorCITB Health and Safety advice for Plant Operators
  • Operators trained, authorised and where applicable certificated in attachment and base unit use
  • Ensure the make and model of attachment is compatible with the base machine
  • Load charts to be available to, and adhered to by operators
  • Fitting and removal of the attachment completed only be authorised and competent operators
  • Attachment to be CE marked
  • Attachment subject to thorough examination intervals as defined under LOLER and PUWER
  • Attachment to be placed upon ground after use
  • Base unit machine to be fitted with ROPS/FOPS
  • Attachment fitted in accordance with manufacturer's instructions
  • No modifications or alterations to take place to the attachment unless authorised by the manufacturer
  • Maintenance completed by competent operators only
  • When grabbed material extends beyond the width of the base unit cab, safe working distances of at least 2 x longest product length must be maintained
  • Operator must not float or suspend loads over persons, plant or structures
  • Operator to complete pre-use check of attachment including hydraulic connections
  • Do not attempt to lift or manoeuvre attachment manually
  • Do not stand between vehicle and walls or implements
  • Observe surroundings when reversing vehicle
  • Maintain a 5m safe zone around the trailed implement and base unit when in operation – stop if anyone enters this area
  • It must be confirmed whether the presence of underground utilities exist prior to the use of ground penetrating machinery i.e. CAT and GENNY, service plans
  • Check the work area to remove material that may be easily ejected by the machine
  • All equipment to be operated from inside the base machine/unit
  • No person must ride or stand on any attachment
  • It should be ensured as a minimum 20% of the base machine and trailed implement weight is over the front axles
  • Operators must ensure when required the correct ballast is applied to the base unit/machine
144
Loading and Unloading of Machinery↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Loading and unloading of machinery1. Unsecured towing vehicle
2. Trailer movement
3. Trailer damage
4. Machine overturn
5. Pinch and crush injuries		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated in machine use
  • Direct supervision of untrained staff and clear briefing to be undertaken before starting
  • Safe level parking of trailers and vehicle
  • Parking brake and trailer brakes applied
  • Where appropriate trailer wheels chocked to prevent roll back
  • Trailer ramp stands, legs or blocks deployed onto firm ground or spreader plates utilised
  • Tracking lever where fitted is checked for direction before moving off
  • Avoid sudden movements during loading, minimise steering
  • Where fitted ROPS must be in place and used, seat belts/restraint always used
  • Members of the public, visitors or helpers are kept clear whilst unloading heavy equipment from vehicles or trailers
  • Ensure adequate Loading/Unloading access to both Vehicle and Loading/Unloading areas
  • Ensure public or other people cannot obstruct access
  • Ensure where possible loading and Unloading takes place Off Road or at least on the Pedestrian side of the carriageway
  • PPE worn as a minimum to include safety boots and gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1)
  • Operational staff trained in safe lifting practices
144
Battery Operated Equipment↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Use of battery-operated equipment including charging1. Injury through faulty equipment use
2. Equipment incompatibility
3. Overheating of equipment
4. Charging equipment or machinery becomes wet
5. Machine in constant live status when battery is engaged
6. Contact with chemicals
7. Misaligned battery – pinch/traps
8. Electric shock		339Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		HSG107 Maintaining portable electrical equipment
INDG231 Electrical safety and you
