INDG139 Using electric storage batteries safely
INDG236 Maintaining portable electric equipment in low-risk environments
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated in the machinery they are using
  • All guards and warnings in place
  • Regular machine maintenance undertaken in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance relative to the machine in use or task being undertaken
  • Damaged or faulty batteries or equipment is not used
  • PAT procedures shall be in place to ensure the safety of electrical equipment
  • Only compatible equipment shall be used as designated by the manufacturer i.e., their charger for their batteries
  • Equipment subject to pre use checks by the operator prior to use
  • Wet equipment and if where batteries are located is wet, they are not to be used until dry
  • No equipment shall be immersed in water or other cleaning substance
  • Charging shall only be done via 240V supply
  • COSHH assessment shall be undertaken to address risks associated with battery acid
  • The company fire risk assessment shall include charging and storage of batteries within the workplace
  • Battery charging equipment shall be stored correctly, without equipment been left hanging from leads
  • The charger must be unplugged if it begins to smoke or catches fire
  • The ventilation slots in the appliance shall be kept clean and clear of debris
  • The appliance shall not be used on a combustible surface
  • The appliance shall not be used in the proximity of volatile chemicals such as fuel
  • The appliance shall not be used in any room which may contain explosive fumes, gasses or dusts
  • Avoid all contact with any substance that appears to be leaking from batteries
  • In case of contact with the skin – wash the affected areas with plenty of water and soap
  • In case of contact with the eyes – rinse the eyes with plenty of water for at least 15 minutes and seek medical advice
  • Batteries and charging equipment must only be maintained and repaired by an authorised service centre
  • Batteries and charging equipment must never be used for any purpose other than the purpose for which they were designed and specified by the manufacturer
  • Dispose of batteries by recycling
  • Never burn batteries or charging equipment
  • Do not pierce, modify, or alter batteries, charging equipment or battery-operated machinery
133
Noise and Vibration↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Noise1. Noise induced hearing loss
2. Deafness
3. Startling of animals		5420Operator
Third parties
Livestock		INDG362 Noise at Work
INDG363 Protect your hearing or lose it
INDG317 Chainsaws at Work
AA Technical Guide 2: Use of Tools in the tree
  • Noise action plan to be implemented
  • Operators are trained in the use, care and storage of their PPE including hearing protection
  • Task/tool rotation to prevent excessive continuous exposure
  • Hearing protection to be worn when within the 5-metre operational area
  • Good communication and awareness when working in loud environments to ensure safety of public and others
  • Equipment not to be left to run in "tick-over" when not in use
  • Only wood to be put in chipper and kept clear of foreign objects
  • Work area assessed for livestock and owners warned before work commences
144
Vibration1. Vibration related injuries5420OperatorINDG175 Control the Risks from Hand-Arm Vibration
INDG296 Hand-Arm Vibration – Advice for Employees
  • The company shall adopt a purchasing policy which considers vibration levels prior to purchase
  • Machinery selected for use shall be fitted with anti-vibration systems
  • Individual machines shall be assessed for their operational vibration levels and the levels shall be recorded on an equipment register
  • Sufficient staff shall be allocated to the team to ensure that operators can share the operation of vibrating equipment
  • Task/tool rotation to prevent excessive continuous exposure
  • Vibration hazards and controls shall be covered during induction training
  • Operators shall receive instruction as to the possible hazards associated with the use of vibrating tools and machinery
  • Correct PPE used to reduce risk of vibration exposure
  • Regular breaks taken from tool operation when operations include long periods of tool use
  • All equipment maintained correctly to create optimum work process and to further reduce risk of excessive vibration and noise
  • Use of heated handle chainsaws within cold environments. And other safety features in place
  • HAV's stage 1 assessments shall be undertaken annually
  • Where stage 1 assessment reveals early signs of vibration injury, advice shall be sought from a doctor specialising in occupational health
144
Manual Handling↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Manual handling tasks including: crosscutting, delimbing, dragging arisings, timber loading, loading machinery such as woodchippers, stumpgrinding (pedestrian controlled), pushing/lifting timber during aerial tree work operations1. Using incorrect handling techniques when handling items
2. Poor posture
3. Individuals with health conditions, previous back injuries etc. affecting ability to safely handle items
4. An individual's physical dimensions and personal capacity
5. Carrying out frequent, long duration manual handling tasks
6. Incorrect handling of aid tools – loss of grip		4416Operator
Work Team
Public		INDG143 Manual handling at work: a brief guide
HSG60 Upper limb disorders in the workplace
INDG417 Chainsaws at Work
AA Technical Guide One: Tree climbing and aerial rescue
AA Technical Guide Two: Safe use of tools in the tree
  • As part of work planning competent persons shall consider if the task of manual handling can either be eliminated or reduced with the provision of mechanical means
  • The production of a manual handling risk assessment shall consider the risks arising from: (a) the task (b) the load (c) the working environment (d) individual capacity (e) any materials handling equipment or handling aids used (f) how we organise and allocate work (g) the pace, frequency and duration of the work
  • A hierarchy of techniques is to be adopted: (a) Avoid lifting (b) Use of mechanical means – crane/skid steer (c) Assisted handling techniques – trolley (d) Manual handling with aid tools (e) Operator manual handling
  • Those individuals in charge of works on site should try to rotate tasks of manual handling between all operators and where manual handling is required, rest breaks are increased
  • Adequate training provided to operators who are required to routinely lift loads as part of their work activities
  • As part of employee induction an individuals personal capacity is assessed, and that person is not assigned moving and handling tasks beyond their ability
  • Where possible loads are split down into smaller loads or a two-person lift is used
  • The operator prior to using aid tools such as timber togs, is to carry out a pre use check to ensure they are fit for use, maintained and if applicable sharp
  • Personal protective equipment including boots with ankle support and good grip, gloves are provided and worn
  • Operators are advised as part of their daily work activities to warm up and stretch prior to starting work
  • The operator shall assess a load prior to lifting to ensure its stability and that a firm grip can be maintained throughout the duration of the lift
  • Under no circumstances is anyone expected to lift anything outside of their personal capacity
  • No load carried is to obscure the individuals vision
  • Good housekeeping is to take place on work sites, in particular the routes used for movement of timber/handling of items is kept clear of debris
  • Operators shall have been briefed on the company health and safety arrangement for manual handling
  • First aid provision shall be available at the point of work
  • Medical declarations completed by all operators as part of employee induction
144
Chainsaw Use↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Fuelling1. Fire/explosion
2. Burns/blast injuries		4416Operator
Work Team
General Public
Client		HSE INDG317 Chainsaws at work
FISA Safety Guide 301 – Using petrol driven chainsaws
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Shaded fuelling site selected
  • Safe distance applied from any ignition source
  • Correct sealed containers used
  • Saw fuel caps replaced securely
144
Maintenance: chain/bar1. Cuts from chain/bar4312Work TeamHSE INDG317 Chainsaws at work
FISA Safety Guide 301 – Using petrol driven chainsaws
  • Avoid bare hand contact with chain and bar
  • Ensure files have handles
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
133
Noise1. Noise Induced Hearing Loss/Deafness4416Operator
Work Team		HSE INDG317 Chainsaws at work
FISA Safety Guide 301
  • Correct saw maintenance (exhaust baffles, chain and bar)
  • Hearing protectors supplied and to be used
  • PPE worn and used correctly – Hearing protection to EN 352-1
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Health surveillance/hearing checks undertaken on employees
144
Vibration1. Vibration-induced White Finger (VWF)
2. Hand Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS)		4416OperatorINDG317 Chainsaws at work
FISA Safety Guide 301
  • Select appropriate equipment to task, operator and environment
  • Monitor and carefully regulate exposure times against published data
  • Correct maintenance for A/V systems, chain and bar
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance – Gloves: Where chainsaw gloves are required these need to be to EN 381-7. Consider the need for protection from cuts from the chainsaw, thorny material and cold/wet conditions
  • Use of manual handling aids to reduce the need to cut large diameter or long lengths of roundwood into manageable pieces
  • Health surveillance/HAVS monitoring
144
Poor handling1. Muscular strain/ long-term injury4416OperatorHSE INDG317 Chainsaws at work
FISA Safety Guide 301–304
  • Avoid manual handling where possible
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Use of ergonomic work systems
  • Work planning considered
144
Contact with moving chain/kickback1. Cut injuries/laceration4520OperatorHSE INDG317 Chainsaws at work
FISA Safety Guide 301, 302, 303, 304, 306, 307
AA Technical Guide Two: Use of tools in the tree
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Saw safety features fitted and working
  • Correct bar and chain maintenance undertaken
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance to include: (a) Safety helmet to EN 397. (b) Hearing protection to EN 352-1. (c) Eye protection: Mesh visors to EN 1731 or safety glasses to EN 166. (d) Gloves: Where chainsaw gloves are required to EN 381-7. (e) Leg protection to EN 381-5 (Type C for aerial treework)
  • Chainsaw boots to BS EN ISO 20345:2004 bearing a shield depicting a chainsaw to show compliance with EN 381-3. Correct bar selection for application
  • Avoid use of kickback area
  • Chain selected with kickback reducing features
155
Tree Felling and Storm Damage – Branch Removal – Crosscutting↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Tree felling1. Impact/crush injuries
2. Moving/rolling timber – traps/crush injuries
3. Loss of directional control
4. Barber chair/splitting		4520Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		HSE INDG317 Chainsaws at work
FISA Safety Guide 301, 302, 303, 304, 306, 307, 310, 501
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Safe working distances applied and maintained
  • The operator is directly responsible for ensuring their felling zone is clear before commencing a felling operation
  • Clear, unambiguous communication procedures are to be in place between operators on site
  • Escape routes maintained
  • Accuracy of cuts accords with industry good practice
  • Use of felling aids as appropriate
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Operator to make an assessment of the material upon which trees may land – felling stopped if timber will become unsecure
  • Felling never carried out where operators are directly below on sloping ground
  • Felling trees affected by Ash Die Back additional controls: Managers must ensure that risk assessments and method statements are effectively communicated to all operators; Operators must be able to identify ADB and Honey Fungus; Only the most competent and well-equipped operators are to be used on sites where dead Ash is prevalent (operator experience to be greater than six months as per FISA 805); The risk assessment process must include justification as to why alternative methodologies such as mechanisation of the task have not been selected first; Avoid using felling aids that may impart shock to the main stem; Operators are to retreat into escape routes as soon as possible; Do not chase a hinge, allow adequate hinge wood to maintain control of the felling direction; It is essential that an operator recognises where weakened timber exists and maintains a stronger hinge than normal. It is also not recommended that an operator bores the centre of the hinge as this again will reduce the amount of effective hinge; Operators must visually inspect the canopy area prior to felling each tree to identify hazards that may pose risk to them and whether it is safe to proceed
  • Assisted Felling: All equipment must be used in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions; Operators are to ensure that all ropes/cables, strops, hooks and eyes etc are suitable for the purpose; No assisted fell operation is to commence unless all required components of the system and all other necessary aids are present; Operators are only to use components that are compatible with each other; Any overload devices such as shear pins must be present; All equipment subject to pre use checks by a proficient operator prior to use to confirm equipment is free from defect and safe for use; Before starting work, operators are to agree a clear and comprehensive communication system. The chainsaw operator must be in control of the operation and give appropriate instructions to the operator engaging the pull system; The assisted felling system chosen must have the capacity and capabilities to pull over the victim tree; Before any cutting begins, the cable or rope is to be correctly attached to a suitable strop placed around the trunk at a point high enough to give adequate leverage; Pull systems shall always be test strained prior to felling operations starting; If cables or ropes run directly from the tree to the pull system, then the distance between the two must be at least twice the height of the tree; Non return pull systems shall always be used during assisted felling operations; Anchor points shall be inspected by a proficient operator and confirmed by that operator as suitable for use; The anticipated norm is that trees are felled using a "hold" and wedges or similar felling aids are used to back up the cuts
155
Delimbing and cross cutting1. Chainsaw injury: Kickback, cut injury/laceration
2. Impact/crush injury
3. Spring back from branches under tension and/or compression
4. Slips and trips		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		HSE INDG317
FISA Safety Guide 301, 303, 304
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Work planning that includes assessment of material that may be pinned under the main stem, and the selection of appropriate cuts to relieve extremes of tension and compression
  • Safe working distances applied and maintained
  • Escape routes maintained by the operator
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • The operator shall carefully assess the tension and compression in the stem and branches continually throughout the operation
  • The operator shall always remain vigilant for signs of movement in the felled stem and in the branches
  • If appropriate, a winch shall be used to prevent the stem from rolling
  • Processing should normally take place with the tree on the right-hand side of the operator
  • Work to be carried out from the upper side of the stem when on slopes
  • Do not use the chainsaw above shoulder height
  • Do not step forward when snedding/delimbing the near side of the stem
  • Maintain a firm grip on both handles of the chainsaw until the chain stops
  • Apply the chain brake before reaching across the guide bar
144
Storm damaged trees: Extreme tension1. Chainsaw injury: Kickback, cut injury/Laceration
2. Impact/crush injury
3. Crush or trap by exposed rootplate		4520Operator
Work Team
Public
Client
Emergency Services Personnel		FISA Safety Guide 302, 304, 306, 310
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Work planning as per FISA 306
  • Safe working distances applied and maintained
  • Escape routes maintained
  • Correct assessment of tension and compression
  • Appropriate sequence of cuts made to sever the main stem – make the final severing cut into the tension wood at least 25mm away from the first (compression) cut and into the part of the stem that is least likely to move
  • Operators are not to crawl under windblown stems
  • Safe use of winch as appropriate
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Staff made aware of safe areas regarding lifted root plates and all specific hazards and possibilities identified as a team before starting work
  • Use of equipment to secure root plate or stem in place, before working on tree
  • No operator is to work beneath an unsecured root plate nor walk through an exposed root bowl
  • Sequence of work will usually mean severing of the root plate takes place first, made safe and then the remaining tree is processed
  • When side tension is present in a stem, operators are to stand on the compression wood side to make the final severing cut
  • At any time when side tension is considerable, the stem is to be restrained with a winch
155
Tree Climbing↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Weak tree structure1. Failure of climber's anchor point
2. Climber falls from tree
3. Limbs fall on climber/ground crew		4520Operator
Work Team		AA TG5: Use of a MEWP in tree work
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
AA TG1: Tree climbing and aerial rescue
  • Avoid climbing: Consider the use of a Mobile Elevating Work Platform (MEWP) as means of access and/or as working platform
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Assessment of anchor points and tree structure (tree condition assessment)
  • Ensure strength of anchor points (2 person or full body weight test when practical)
  • Use of a personal fall protection system that includes a primary system and back up, connected to anchors which are independent of each other; if there is no suitable second anchor, systems are installed over a shared anchor
155
Equipment failure1. Climber falls from or within tree
2. Major or minor contact injuries		4520Operator
Work Team		AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
AA TG1
  • Pre-use checks of equipment undertaken
  • Use of a personal fall protection system that includes a primary system and back up, connected to anchors which are independent of each other; if there is no suitable second anchor, systems are installed over a shared anchor
  • Worn/damaged equipment withdrawn from service
  • Equipment inspected regularly by a competent person
  • Thorough Examination of Lifting Equipment (PPE climbing equipment every 6 months)
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Manufacturers instruction manual/operators made available to all operators
155
Poor climbing technique: Free climbing, unprotected position, loss of balance1. Climber falls from, or within, tree
2. Major or minor contact injuries		4520Operator
Work Team		AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
AA TG1
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Use of correct equipment and procedures
  • Use of a personal fall protection system that includes a primary system and back up, connected to anchors which are independent of each other; if there is no suitable second anchor, systems are installed over a shared anchor
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Operators must always remain attached to a load bearing anchor point
155
Falling objects: equipment, tools, branches1. Injury to persons and/or property below4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
AA TG1
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Correct use of tools, equipment, and tool strops
  • Identified hazard areas – include identification and demarcation of drop zones
  • No groundstaff to enter an identified drop zone without verbal consent from the climber
  • Climber directly responsible for providing a verbal warning prior to casting anything from a tree and only upon receipt of a positive verbal confirmation from groundstaff that the drop zone is clear
  • As part of the SSRA process all staff must agree a method of communication between them that is clear and unambiguous
  • Equipment should be connected to the climber's harness prior to its release from any system set up to haul equipment into the tree
  • Groundstaff shall not install items such as lowering devices whilst the climber ascends the tree or is working directly overhead
  • The climber is responsible for providing a warning to groundstaff and ensuring the area directly below is clear prior to installing hardware such as pulleys, cambium savers etc
144
Awkward movements when working in tree1. Muscular strain, back injury4416OperatorAA Tree Work at Height ICoP
AA TG1
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Planned work programme
  • Ergonomic climbing techniques, and routing of ropes to achieve best working positions
  • Use of supplementary anchors
  • Operators are actively encouraged to maintain a level of personal fitness alongside "warming up" prior to starting work
144
Chainsaw in the Tree including Dismantling↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Top-handled saw use by climber1. Kickback, cut injury/laceration
2. Cutting climbing rope causing fall injury/fatality		4416Operator
Work Team		HSE INDG317
FISA Safety Guide 301
AA TG1, TG2
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Top handled saws used only in tree (apart from warm-up)
  • Operator in correct position
  • Ropes kept in safe position
  • Use of supplementary anchor
  • Use of cut-resistant lanyard if applicable
  • Chain brake applied after cutting
  • Two-handed use of the chainsaw always
  • Manufacturers instruction manual/operators made available to all operators
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
144
Passing saw to or from climber1. Saw falling on ground staff
2. Rope damage		4312Operator
Work Team		HSE INDG317
FISA Safety Guide 301
AA TG1, TG2
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Good communication established between operators
  • Correct methods of attaching/securing saw used
  • Use of tool line if appropriate
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Groundstaff to move from drop zone prior to aerial operator hauling equipment
  • Aerial operator to provide verbal warning to ground staff prior to disconnecting equipment from a rope system i.e. climbing line
133
Large saw use1. Kickback, cut injury/laceration
2. Vibration
3. Muscular strain, back injury
4. Cutting climbing rope/lanyard leading to slip or fall injury/fatality		4416Operator
Work Team		HSE INDG317
FISA Safety Guide 301
AA TG1, TG2, TG5
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Saw is appropriate to task
  • Saw supported centrally on harness or on tool line if appropriate
  • During saw use operator in correct position
  • Ropes kept in safe position
  • Use of supplementary anchor
  • Use of cut-resistant lanyard if applicable
  • Chain brake applied after cutting
  • Two-handed use always
  • Manufacturers instruction manual/operators made available to all operators
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
144
Branch removal1. Cuts, impact/crush injuries4312Operator
Work Team
General Public
Client		HSE INDG317
FISA/AFAG Safety Guide 301
AA TG1, TG2, TG3, TG5
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Climber to achieve a safe work position during branch removal whereby they will not be struck by moving timber
  • Climber carefully considers the route any severed piece of timber may take and is responsible for ensuring measures are taken so that the climber nor any part of the system shall be struck
  • Accurate severing cuts used
  • Team communication established and maintained
  • Work equipment, plant and machinery stored securely at suitable distance from tree
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Client/Public excluded from work area
  • Drop zone kept clear during any free fall operations
  • Climber directly responsible for providing a verbal warning prior to casting or free falling anything from a tree and only upon receipt of a positive verbal confirmation from groundstaff that the drop zone is clear
133
Use of lowering equipment1. System failure, impact/crush injuries
2. Rope friction burns		4312Operator
Work Team		AA TG1, TG2, TG3, TG5
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
  • Control measures specified for aerial tree work (tree climbing and use of chainsaw in the tree) are to be adopted alongside this dismantling risk assessment
  • Tree climbing operations and the use of tools in the tree are to be carried out in accordance with Technical Guide One: Tree climbing and aerial rescue, Technical Guide Two: Safe use of tools in the tree
  • Specific reference must be made by operators to Technical Guide 3 to ensure that any dismantling operation is carried out in accordance with industry good practice
  • Dismantling operations must be planned by a competent person and only carried out by a proficient operator with appropriate levels of skill, knowledge, and experience in the type of work to be carried out
  • All operators to be adequately trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Equipment subject to pre-use checks by a proficient operator
  • Any item of defective equipment identified to be removed from service immediately
  • Equipment shall be inspected regularly by a proficient operator
  • Thorough Examination of Lifting Equipment (lowering equipment every 12 months) carried out by a competent person
  • Correct routing of ropes by the climber in the tree so not to contact any part of the personal fall protection system
  • The selection of anchor points by a proficient operator must be able to withstand the loads it shall experience
  • Peak loads must be within the Safe Working Load of equipment in use
  • Team communication maintained throughout dismantling operations
  • Groundstaff to wear gloves when handling lowering ropes; rope must never be wrapped around an operator's hand or arm
  • It must be ensured at least 5m of rope remains 'spare' after a lowering operation has been completed
  • Only those operators adequately trained in the use of lowering devices shall set them up and use them
  • The selection of components by a proficient operator to make up any dismantling system must ensure those items remain correctly configured during the task, are compatible with each other and are designed for the purpose for which the operator intends to use them
133
Use of Handheld Pruning Tools↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Pruning tools (secateurs, loppers)1. Injury due to twisting and bending
2. Nip/pinches in hinge mechanism
3. Cuts to fingers and in extreme cases amputation of fingers in blades
4. Cuts/abrasions on hand/fingers
5. Injury due to falling over secateurs/loppers
6. Injury from falling over while carrying or injury to another person hit by tool
7. Repetitive strain to wrists and arms
8. Dust, debris and plant toxins causing eye, skin and respiratory irritation
9. Injury through projecting branches: Scratches and penetrating injury to face and eyes		4312OperatorFISA 806 - Welfare
  • Equipment checked for defects prior to use. If a defect is found, then the tool must be discarded, and line manager informed
  • Use of gloves (min EN388 4.3.3.1). Keep hand away from cutting blades. Always use both handles on loppers
  • Do not use secateurs/loppers for any other purpose than that listed above
  • When not in use the secateurs/loppers should be guarded
  • Carry secateurs/loppers in hand held down to the side of the body. Do not carry too many tools at once
  • Safe working distances as determined by the site risk assessment must be established and maintained between operators and third parties
  • Always carry out good hygiene practices e.g. Washing and covering cuts with a sterile dressing, washing hands after work and prior to eating and drinking
  • Preventative maintenance undertaken in accordance with manufacturers handbook/guidance
  • Never use the wrong tool for the job e.g. a knife instead of pruners
  • Always use the correct size of equipment for the thickness of the material to be pruned e.g. loppers
  • Always clean tools after use and store in a tidy and secure manner
  • Never leave tools as tripping hazard
  • Never use cutting tools e.g. knives towards the hand
  • To avoid injury never use damaged, blunt or broken tools
133
Tree Planting↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Tree Planting1. Contact with branches and twigs
2. Contact with soil borne organisms
3. Digging and moving earth
4. Manual handling of bags of plants
5. Slips, trips and falls
6. Contact with underground services		4416OperatorHSE HSG47 Avoiding danger from underground services
  • All workers will be warned of risk of scratches, safe working distance will be kept between workers
  • Broken skin must be covered with waterproof plaster before work starts
  • Cuts received will be washed and treated immediately
  • Wash hands before eating, drinking and smoking
  • Wear protective gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1) if working in or moving soil by hand
  • Use tools and feet to move soils, and to firm soils around plants
  • Work must be rotated to allow rest from more strenuous tasks
  • All operators trained in safe lifting and handling techniques
  • Provision of wheelbarrows and other handling aids to move plants and tools over longer distances
  • Tools to be left in clear view and in safe position, site trip hazards will be notified to all operators
  • Use of CAT/GENNY and/or trial holes to aid in the identification of underground services
144
Tree Inspection↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Conducting a tree inspectionDamaged, unstable or broken trees/branches resulting in: 1. Crush injury; 2. Bruising
Needles, broken glass or other sharp items causing: 1. Puncture wound; 2. Cuts; 3. Viral infection
Water (rivers, ponds, wells) resulting in: 1. Drowning; 2. Hypothermia		4520Tree Inspector
Public
Client		AA Aerial Inspections: Guide to Good Practice
  • Initial and ongoing site inspection, known hazards identified, marked, or taped off as appropriate
  • Use of appropriate PPE where necessary
  • Stop work if weather deteriorates and creates significant risks e.g. high winds
  • Use probes or other tools to investigate cavities or decay
  • Clear debris or undergrowth around trees with appropriate tools
  • Leave any needles undisturbed and report to site owner or Environmental Health Service
  • Identify water hazards prior to starting work
  • If working close to water is unavoidable, avoid lone working and ensure means of rescue available
  • Establish safe traffic routes
  • Emergency procedures must be prepared, recorded and communicated
  • Lifejacket – harness used on ground sloping towards water where firm footing is not guaranteed
  • Use of appropriate PPE e.g. boot with good grip and ankle support
155
Blower/Vacuum/Sucker↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Handheld and backpack blower, vacuum, sucker1. Slips and trips using machinery
2. Fire/explosion – burns, blast injuries
3. Cuts, traps, caught in moving parts
4. Hit by material
5. Noise - deafness
6. Vibration – HAVS
7. Dust – eye injury
8. Upper limb disorders or lower back injury from repetitive machinery use
9. Contact with biotic factors and biohazards		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA 802 – Emergency planning
  • Adequate training in machine use received by operators
  • Operators shall receive manual handling training
  • First aid kit, spill kit and fire extinguisher all available in proximity during machinery use
  • Gloves worn during maintenance activities to prevent cuts and personal contamination
  • Good personal hygiene maintained by operators including regular hand washing, hand cleansing wipes available within vehicles
  • COSHH assessments readily available for operators to reference
  • Safe-stop procedures adopted prior to removing blockages
  • Machine operated in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • The machine shall be maintained by the operator in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Correct assembly of air discharge components
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance: (a) A safety helmet (b) Hearing protection (c) Eye protection (d) Gloves suitable for the intended task (e) Protective boots with good grip and ankle support (f) Non-snag outer clothing appropriate to the prevailing weather conditions (g) High visibility clothing
  • Safe working zones established and agreed (at least 10m)
  • Effective communication established between all parties
  • Pre-work checks undertaken to confirm machine suitability
  • Guards fitted and present during machine operation
  • Fuel is positioned at least 10m from the work area
  • Proactive spill provision used
  • Machinery allowed to cool prior to refuelling
  • Operators to stop work if the 10m buffer zone is breached
  • Operators are not to blow material directly at property that could become damaged such as doors, cars, windows etc.
  • Machinery should be used at low revs to gather material into smaller piles
  • Operators are never to point machinery at other people
  • The vibration magnitude of the machinery shall be made known to operators and any applicable maximum exposure times for machine use
  • Needles and other biohazards found within material to be cleared shall not be handled by staff unless specifically trained and authorised to do so
133
Brushwood Chipper↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Hand or machine fed Woodchipper1. Cuts, noise (deafness)
2. Entanglement
3. Limb/digit injury/severance
4. Eye injury
5. MSDs related to manual handling of material
6. Noise and dust
7. Collisions with persons and property
8. Contact with energised lines – electrocution
9. Machine overturn – crush/trap
10. Machine overload or attachment failure causing crush or impact injury
11. Machinery damage from falling debris
12. Struck by boom, chassis, moving load
13. Load collides with other plant
14. Material falls and contacts operators/other personnel		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA Safety Guide 604 Woodchipper
FISA Safety Guide 802, 804, 805, 806
HSE INDG362, INDG363, INDG143
HSE HSG47
HSE GS6
  • Hand Fed:
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • The client and any other party who may be directly affected by dust and noise shall be informed prior to starting work
  • Machinery is only to be used for its intended purpose i.e. chipping. It shall not be used as a pulling implement
  • A safe working zone of at least 5m shall be maintained around the machine, an area within which no third party shall be allowed to enter – use of banksman, signs tape and/or fixed barriers shall be used to achieve this
  • The operator is responsible for inspecting material prior to chipping to ensure foreign objects are removed
  • Manufacturers instruction manual/operators made available to all operators
  • Machinery is only to be ridden on, where a footplate is fitted to the machine
  • Where fitted the "emergency stop bar" and all other guards, and warnings must be in place and fully functional
  • Regular machine maintenance undertaken; Safe fuelling and storage of fuel
  • PPE worn shall conform to the requirements of FISA 604 and include the use of non-snag outer clothing and close-fitting gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1)
  • The operators feeding position shall be to the side of the machine and directly behind the infeed hopper
  • No part of the operator's body must enter the infeed hopper or be placed into areas where moving parts exist
  • Blockages must be dealt with strictly in accordance with manufacturers guidance, which must include safe stop procedures
  • No part of an operator's body may be used to push material into the feed hopper, a push stick of at least 1.5m in length is to be used
  • The operator shall select the machines tracking speed, relative to the terrain ground conditions
  • Where fitted variable track width is to be used to maintain machine stability
  • The maximum permissible incline as stated within manufacturers guidance is not to be exceeded
  • Machinery shall not be used to carry passengers
  • Hand cleaning facilities and first aid provision to be available at the point of work
  • Operators should only lift material within their own personal capacity; larger material should be broken down into manageable lengths and where possible the machine moved closer to where material is to be processed minimising manual handling
  • Machine Fed:
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • The operator's position shall always be from inside the cab
  • Manufacturers instruction manual/operators made available to all operators
  • Where fitted all guards, and warnings must be in place and fully functional
  • Orange and green beacons shall be fitted and in use during machine operation
  • Regular machine maintenance shall be undertaken
  • Unauthorised personnel excluded from work area: signs, barriers, etc. Exclusion zones must include the discharge and infeed areas
  • No work within 15m of any overhead power line without contacting Regional Electricity Company supplier (REC)
  • Undertaking of visual site inspection prior to starting work to identify presence of UG or OH infrastructure
  • The operator is responsible for monitoring grab movements during use
  • Means of contacting REC/Distribution Network Operator is known to all staff
  • No person shall walk beneath the discharge chute or feeding arm during operation
  • Material shall never be directed at any person or object other than the area chip is intended to land on or in
  • PPE when outside the cab shall include non-snag outer clothing, hi visibility vest, safety helmet, protective boots
  • The machine is never hand fed
  • Pre use checks to determine machine is safe for use carried out by the operator
  • The machine is set up in accordance with the manufacturer's guidance including the deployment of stabiliser legs where fitted and the cab used in an elevated position where fitted
  • The height of the cab shall be set according to operator preference to provide good all-round visibility during chipping
  • The loading tray shall be fully lowered and in final position prior to loading material
  • Material for processing shall be subject to a visual inspection by the operator to ensure hazards such as wire, cables, metal work or other foreign objects are not present, and the material is safe to process
  • The operator must only lift material within the capacity of the grab
  • Where the grab cannot hold material securely, material shall be lowered, and the grab repositioned
  • Material is only fed within the infeed capacity of the machine
  • The grab is used for no other purpose than to feed material into the machine
  • Material is let go once taken by the infeed mechanism
  • The discharge deflector plate is positioned to minimise the risks associated with flying debris
144
Stump Grinder↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Stump grinder1. Injury to operator due to being unfamiliar with machine
2. Slips and trips
3. Collision with vehicle
4. Cuts
5. Noise (deafness)
6. Injury from flying debris
7. Injury from contact with cutter wheel
8. Contact with underground services – electrocution
9. Fire, explosion – burns
10. Vibration exposure – HAVS
11. Upper limb disorders owing to repetitive nature of task
12. Crush injuries – machine overturn
13. Contact with biohazards – sharps, animal faeces
14. Manual handling – heavy equipment, awkward posture, soil/spoil movement		4520Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA Safety Guide 606 Stumpgrinders
FISA Safety Guide 802, 805, 806
HSE INDG 362, 363
HSE HSG47 Avoiding danger from underground services
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Operators are to be trained in appropriate manual handling techniques that includes loading and unloading of machinery
  • Operators are to be trained in the risks associated with underground services, how to identify and locate their presence and safe working practices to be adopted prior to breaking ground
  • During work planning equipment that eliminates operator fatigue (hydrostatic/self-propelled, auto sweep) must be considered initially as a work method, prior to selecting machines that require physical operator input
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual shall be made available to all operators
  • The vibration magnitude of the machine and maximum exposure time shall be made known to operators
  • Guidance outlined in the New Road and Street Works Act shall be adopted to ensure the protection of the operator when working within or proximity to a highway
  • A walk through of the worksite by the operator prior to starting work should look for evidence of sharps or other biohazards – work shall not be started until hazards are removed (by a trained, competent operator only)
  • All machine guards and warnings must be in place
  • Regular machine maintenance undertaken
  • Safe fuelling and storage of fuel, out of direct sunlight, away from naked flames, allowing a machine to cool prior to refuelling, at least 5m from a watercourse
  • Fixed barriers (grinder guard, curtain, screens) must be in place to prevent flying debris
  • All personnel including on site operatives, excluded from work area: signs, barriers, etc.
  • PPE worn by operators and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance AFAG 606
  • Safe working practices are to be adopted by the operator in line with AFAG 606
  • Use of CAT/GENNY and/or trial holes to aid in the identification of underground services
  • An operator using a CAT must be adequately trained in its use and the machine must be within its calibration date
  • Under no circumstances shall any part of an operator's body be used to slow a cutting wheel
  • The cutting wheel shall be bought to a full stop if the machine is to be repositioned or the backfilling of a hole required
  • The operator shall remove immediately obvious hazards from around the stump such as stones, prior to grinding
155
Winches↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Winches – hand or mechanical1. Failure of winch system caused by overloading
2. Failure of winch system caused by inadequate or incorrect maintenance
3. Incorrect use of equipment
4. Manual handling of winch and cable
5. Struck by object/equipment in use
6. Pinch/cuts from cable		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA Safety Guide 310 Use of winches in directional felling and takedown
FISA Safety Guide 502 Extraction by skidder
FISA Safety Guide 802, 805, 806
Forestry Commission Winching operations in forestry – tree takedown and vehicle debogging
  • Operators are to be trained, competent where applicable and authorised to carry out winching operations
  • Ensure winch and associated cables and slings have adequate pulling capacity for task
  • Safe working load should be clearly visible on winch
  • All components in the winch system are to be compatible, especially in respect of their safe working loads (SWL), and that any safety devices (e.g. shear pins) are fitted correctly
  • Use only equipment which has an up-to-date inspection certificate
  • Do not use cables and slings with damaged wires, kinks, or visible centre fibres
  • Pre use checks of all equipment undertaken by the operator before each use
  • Equipment maintained according to manufacturer's instructions
  • Store equipment in dry conditions
  • Clean and dry dirty cables and slings after use
  • Slings should not be connected to rusting hooks
  • Where practical store cables coiled onto suitable drums
  • All equipment must be used in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions
  • Before starting work, a clear and comprehensive communication system must be in place. The chainsaw operator must be in control of the operation and give appropriate instructions to the winch operator. However, an emergency stop signal must be obeyed by all parties immediately. Any unclear signal should be regarded as a stop signal
  • If conditions prevent the chainsaw operator's clear sight of the winch operator, then an alternative method, such as a radio, or the use of a third person to relay the message must be used
  • PPE shall include – Safety helmet (complying with EN 397), Eye protection (mesh visor complying with EN 1731 or safety glasses to EN 166), Hearing protection (complying with EN 352), if working with or near a tractor, Suitable work gloves, Protective boots with good grip and ankle support (complying with EN 345-1), Non-snag outer clothing, High-visibility clothing when working on or near the public highway or when working with other machinery
  • Each person should carry a personal first-aid kit including a large wound dressing
  • Warning signs shall be positioned at least twice the length of the cable being used in all directions
  • Ensure that the winch, cables, and slings are free from obstructions
  • Ensure that there is a clear line of pull so that the load can be directed accurately
  • Attach winch cable to load via an appropriate sling/chain to avoid kinks in the cable
  • Anchor points should be suitable for the load to be pulled, appropriate selection of anchor points in terms of size, strength, and stability
  • Ensure that only one person operates winch
  • When using a tractor, pull using the winch, never with the tractor
  • Never leave tensioned winch systems unattended
  • Plan work to minimise the distance the winch and cables need to be transported
  • When equipment is to be carried, provide carrying aids, e.g. wheelbarrow
  • Operators shall wear gloves when handling cables and wire slings
  • During felling operations if the cable(s) run directly from the tree to the winch then the distance between the two must be at least twice the height of the tree
  • Do not operate the winch from a position which is too close to the butt being winched, or below the butt on a steep slope. A snatch block should be used to offset the cable to ensure a safe working position
  • When an offset is used, the additional force exerted on the redirect anchor must be considered to ensure components selected have an adequate SWL
  • When winching downhill, where the tree may move towards the winch, it must always be offset from the danger area
  • Remain in the tractor cab or in a designated safe operating position until tension has been released from the winch cable
  • Do not unhitch the cable until the tree is in a stable position on the ground
  • During offset winching operations, no operator must walk or work inside the triangle 'danger zone' created by using such a system
144
Brushcutter/Trimmer/Clearing Saw↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Brushcutter and Strimmer1. Operator unfamiliar with machinery
2. Cuts, noise (deafness)
3. Injury from flying debris
4. Injury from contact with cutting head/blade
5. Personal contamination – fuel and oils
6. Slips, trips and falls
7. Noise Induced Hearing Loss
8. Hand arm vibration disorders
9. Contact with hazardous chemical and biological substances
10. Upper limb disorders from repetitive nature of machinery use		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA Safety Guide 203 – Clearing saw
FISA Safety Guide 802, 805, 806
HSE INDG362, INDG363
HSE INDG296 Hand-Arm Vibration
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Operators are encouraged to warm up and stretch prior to using machinery
  • Regular machine maintenance undertaken with safe stop procedures adopted and the correct use of PPE such as gloves if handling blades
  • Only machinery attachments approved by the manufacturer are to be used – no modifications or alterations to be carried out
  • All equipment subject to pre use checks by the operator to confirm equipment is safe for use
  • The operator is responsible using the pictograms marked on the machine and the operator's manual that they are using the correct guard for the cutting head in use
  • Blade speed must not exceed the machines RPM as displayed on both the cutting head and machine
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Safe working distance of at least 15m is to be maintained between operators
  • Safe fuelling and storage of fuel – out of direct sunlight, spill mat used, stored at least 5m from watercourses, no smoking
  • Correct fitting of harness ensured, harness always used – fitted by the operator, checked by the team leader
  • Unauthorised personnel excluded from work area: signs, barriers, etc.
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance including a polycarbonate/clear visor for trimming or safety glasses and mesh visor
  • First aid kit available at point of work
  • Work activity to stop if safe working distances breached by any party
  • Walk through of site undertaken prior to work starting to move or mark loose debris
  • Clearance of vegetation around metal posts or stanchions only carried out using nylon cord or poly cut heads
  • When working around obstructions either use the guard to act as a barrier between the cutting head and obstruction, or use the machine on very low revs, gently feathering the throttle
  • Mowing of material should be carried out in a direction to deposit material back onto an already cut area and never into a highway
  • Footpaths shall be cleared of debris prior to leaving site
  • Machinery never left unattended; No smoking during machinery operation or fuelling
  • Health screening of operators shall be carried out to monitor the effects of vibration exposure
  • Vibration magnitude levels and maximum exposure time shall be known to operators
  • Operators work pattern should minimise movements continually up and down slopes, ideally working across the face of a slope
133
Hedgetrimmers↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Hedge trimming1. Noise - deafness
2. Injury from contact with cutting knives
3. Personal contamination – fuel and oils
4. Upper limb disorders associated with using long reach or extendable trimmer
5. Manual handling – weight of machine/awkward movement
6. Flying debris – eye injury
7. Dust – eye injury
8. Contact with foreign object/entanglement in blade
9. Trimmed material falling onto operator
10. Contact with OHPL or utility such as telecoms cable
11. Overreaching leading to the step ladder toppling over
12. Biohazards
13. Refuelling – fire and explosion		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		Arboricultural Association ICOP: Tree Work at Height
AA Technical Guide One, Two
FISA Safety Guide 802, 804, 805, 806
HSE INDG 362, 363, INDG296
LA455 Safe Use of Ladders and Stepladders – a brief guide
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • PPE shall include as a minimum safety footwear, helmet with ear and eye protection, non-snag outer clothing and gloves suitable for the intended task
  • Operators are advised to warm up prior to work to reduce the risks associated with upper limb disorders
  • Regular machine maintenance undertaken
  • Operators to be made aware of machinery vibration magnitude levels and any maximum exposure times for use
  • Machinery selected for use shall be fit for purpose in that it is designed for hedge trimming work
  • Pre use checks of machine carried out by the operator to confirm safety features are present and function, machine is safe to operate
  • Prior to cutting, the material to be cut is inspected for foreign objects such as wire to ensure the sequence of work avoids such hazards
  • Safe fuelling and storage of fuel to be adopted
  • Machine is used with two hands; Adjustments are not made to the machine whilst it is running
  • The set up of the machine should include the correct angling of cutting heads
  • Long reach machinery is positioned so the operator is not directly in the drop zone of material
  • Unauthorised personnel excluded from work area: signs, barriers, etc.
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with manufacturers guidance and/or site specific risk assessment
  • No part of the operator's body is to touch moving or hot components
  • Operators are not to over-reach with the machine during use
  • A safe working distance of at least 5m is to be maintained between operators during machine use
  • COSHH assessments carried out considering likely material to be encountered
  • First aid provision available at worksite
  • Handheld machinery should generally be restricted to use at or below chest height
  • Work at height shall not be carried out unless planned by a competent person, no makeshift step ups, or work at height platforms are to be used
  • If machinery is to be used in or on top of a hedge the operator is to be secured via a personal fall protection system and must always be secured to at least one load bearing anchor
  • Leaning ladders are not to be used to carry out hedge trimming tasks
  • Work at height assessment must consider achieving the desired results from ground level, prior to the use of a temporary work platform
  • The use of step ladders to carry out hedge trimming work may be considered appropriate when: (a) Duration of work to be less than 30 mins (b) Light work to be undertaken (Less than or equal to 10kg) (c) Space available to fully open ladders (d) Any locking devices can be used properly (e) Pre-use check completed, and item deemed safe for use (f) Ground found to be firm and level (g) Side on working can be avoided (h) Work can be completed not using the top two steps
133
Mechanised Pole Pruner↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Mechanised pole pruner1. Cuts, noise (deafness)
2. Injury from contact with chain, severed sections falling on operator/others
3. Dust/fumes
4. Vibration – HAVS
5. Strike with overhead utility – electrocution		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Regular machine maintenance undertaken
  • Pre use checks of machine carried out by the operator to confirm safety features are present and function, machine is safe to operate
  • Safe working distances of at least 15m maintained between operators
  • Safe working distance of at least 15m maintained from any OHPL
  • Safe fuelling and storage of fuel
  • Observe working height/angle limits which are not to be exceeded during use
  • Unauthorised personnel excluded from work area: signs, barriers, etc.
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Branch removal carried out using appropriate techniques such as step cuts or through cuts on small diameter timber
  • Preference shown for the use on inboard step cut free fall
  • Release of a trapped machine ensures no other operator can be struck as a machine is released
144
Mobile Elevating Work Platform (MEWP)↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Mobile elevating work platform (MEWP) – use of tools and equipment from the MEWP1. Injury due to operator not being familiar with the equipment in use
2. Fall from MEWP
3. Failure of harness attachment points
4. MEWP overturning/toppling (unstable ground/voids)
5. MEWP overturn due to debris striking outrigger
6. MEWP overturning/toppling (exceeding SWL)
7. Inclement weather – damage to machine, property, machine overturn, operators suffer from wind exposure
8. Contact with OHPL
9. Crush injuries between base unit and obstructions		4520Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		AA TG5: Use of a MEWP in tree work
C&G NPTC/Lantra Awards L2/3 Competencies
HSE information sheet MISC 614: Preventing falls from boom-type mobile elevating work platforms
HSE GS6 Avoidance of danger from overhead electric power lines
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Pre use checks of the machine by the operator must include a walk around and reference to the manual to ensure that they understand the controls and functions of the machine type in use
  • A site survey must be carried out to establish suitability/strength of ground for MEWP outriggers
  • Checks made for unstable, loose ground, culverts, gullies, inspection chambers/covers, soft ground, service ducts etc.
  • Outriggers are never to be placed on kerb edges, manholes, voids, covered drains, ditches, open voids etc
  • Use of suitable load spreaders as determined by site and ground conditions survey, referring to manufacturers/suppliers' information
  • Selection of suitable MEWP type/size as dictated by site conditions and task
  • MEWP to have full and intact guard-rails being "fit for purpose" and fully LOLER'd with current certificate supplied
  • Operator to stay within confines of MEWP "bucket" (unless transferring into tree using recognised procedure TG5)
  • Use of appropriate personal fall prevention system (work restraint)
  • Operators must always stay secured to lanyard attachment point (unless transferring to the tree) and only use approved attachment points for securing lanyard
  • Operators to carry out a visual observation of attachment point to ensure security and to check for signs of deformation
  • Ensure bucket is clearly marked with safe working load (SWL)
  • The MEWP is to be operated within its SWL
  • Do not attach unauthorised items to MEWP bucket, i.e. rigging/lowering systems
  • Do not use the MEWP as a crane to lift/lower loads
  • Functionality of emergency descent controls confirmed prior to the machine being used in a raised position
  • Designated individual (who remains on the ground) is assigned to co-ordinate emergency descent if required
  • Where the manufacturers stated windspeed is exceeded work must stop
  • The operator shall be equipped with an anemometer to check the wind speed as required
  • The machine (at full extension) shall not be used within 15m of OHPL cables unless consent has been obtained from the relevant DNO
  • A distance of at least 500mm shall be left between the base unit and any other obstruction
  • The positioning of the MEWP shall be so that it does not extend over unprotected highways, railways or OHPL's
  • Use of tools and equipment: The setup of the machine must attempt to position the base unit and outriggers a distance from material to be cut to minimise any risk of striking the machine; No two operators shall occupy the same MEWP basket when using chainsaws unless separated by a physical barrier; Machinery such as chainsaws and hedge trimmers are to be used by trained and certificated operators; Machinery shall be used in accordance with both the specific risk assessment for that machine type and relevant industry good practice; Where reasonably practical machinery shall be tethered to the basket using a lanyard that has been subject to thorough examination; Operators are not to overreach when using tools and equipment; Operators must not attempt to gain extra height in the basket such as using steps or standing on a guard rail; A distance of at least 150mm should be left between the edge of the basket and material to be cut; Prior to any material free falling or being cast from the basket the operator is responsible for ensuring a warning call/command has been given and a response that it is safe to proceed from groundcrew has been received; Where a prolonged available drop zone is required such as during hedge cutting a drop zone should be physically marked e.g. tape and cones, and access to groundstaff prevented until a command has been given by the aerial operator that it is safe to proceed; Operators should cut small, manageable amounts of material to maintain control and direction; Refuelling of machinery shall never be carried out at height
155
Pesticide Application↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Pesticide Application1. Contamination of operator, Poisoning, over or under application
2. Poisoning of operators or the public, illness, death
3. Contamination of operator resulting in illness
4. Contact with chemicals, illness, death
5. Spray drift, causing unintended contact with either people or property
6. Contamination of the environment, poisoning		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		AIS 16 Guidance on storing pesticides for farmers and other professional users
HSE/DEFRA Code of practice for using plant protection products
FISA 806 - Welfare
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Only use approved pesticides
  • Ensure that pesticides are stored in a fixed, designated, locked and bunded container marked with a logo depicting an exclamation mark
  • When transporting pesticides, containers should be stored in a sealed, vapour proof box containing a general danger warning sign. Pesticides should never be transported in the cab of a vehicle
  • Pesticides should be locked in a vapour proof box when unattended
  • Pesticide containers should display an original label and should not be reused, save for transfer into another container of the same product (to deal with leaking containers)
  • Washings should be diluted and used on the spray site unless the maximum dosage has been reached in which case, washings should be disposed of on a similar site
  • Containers awaiting disposal should be stored in a locked, dry area preferably separate to the chemical store carrying the exclamation mark logo
  • Empty containers should be disposed of using a licensed disposal contractor. Unopened pesticide containers may be returned to the supplier if the supplier is agreeable
  • PPE should be stored in a separate ventilated locker. Related equipment (i.e. brushes) should be stored in a segregated area
  • PPE should be checked before, during and after use and maintained at regular intervals. Maintenance records should be kept
  • Contaminated/damaged PPE should be stored with the empty pesticide containers and disposed of using a licensed disposal contractor
  • All related equipment should be rinsed thoroughly and decontaminated before storage. Gloves and face masks/shields must also be washed thoroughly. The waste water should be disposed of in the same manner as the washings
  • Only operatives with a regulated qualification/licence to practice may use spraying equipment or treat stumps. To treat or apply pesticides near a water course, operatives must hold the relevant qualification (application near water)
  • Operators should observe the Codes of Practice for safe use of pesticides
  • The correct PPE, namely coveralls (CE marked, type 5 approved, splash resistant), Nitrile gloves (CE marked, 300mm in length, 0.5mm thick, non-absorbent), rubber wellington boots and a face shield should be worn at all times when handling and mixing pesticides
  • Ensure all PPE is removed, and hands are washed thoroughly before eating, drinking and smoking and do not eat, drink or smoke near the spray site
  • Always read the product label before use and adhere to any COSHH assessment
  • If personal contamination is suspected, wash in cold water immediately, remove contaminated clothing and secure in a tagged bag and seek immediate medical treatment
  • If contamination of another person is suspected, put on the correct PPE, remove the person from the site, decontaminate with cold water and seek immediate medical treatment
  • Always carry a first aid kit and eye wash
  • Use signs to warn members of the public who may be using the site. Limit access to the area being treated (note, public rights of way cannot be closed without authority)
  • Ensure sprayed areas are cordoned or marshalled off for two hours after application
  • Perform Environment Risk Assessment for Pesticides to highlight potential risks on treatment site and assess if conditions are suitable for spraying
  • Where possible, ensure that the sprayer is transported empty and filled on site. If this is not possible, ensure that sprayers are secured and stored up right
  • Ensure that the filling/mixing point is at least 5m from any watercourses and 1m buffer zones are observed when using a knapsack sprayer or stump treating near a watercourse
  • Do not exceed the recommended dose rates or the manufacturers' recommendations
  • Only pesticides recommended for use "in or near water" may be used in water or on river banks
  • When stump treating, use a dye where appropriate
  • Never treat stumps or spray in wet weather or during high winds as the chances of spray drift are increased
  • Ensure no livestock will be present for the recommended time
  • At times of the year when bees are at risk of contamination, alter spray times and contact the Local Beekeepers Association (Spray Liaison Officer) 48 hours in advance of any spraying activity to request that beekeepers close their hives
  • Ensure all related equipment is removed from site
  • Any spillages should be dealt with using a spill kit containing a brush, shovel, absorbent granules and impenetrable container and disposed of using a licensed disposal contractor
  • Operators to be aware that fully loaded knapsack sprayers are heavy
  • Use correct manual handling procedures to move a loaded sprayer and where possible, place the knapsack sprayer on a raised surface (not a picnic bench) and reverse into the straps
  • Suitable and sufficient records (application and stock) must be kept
133
Compact Loading Machine↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Operating a 'compact' loading machine1. Unexpected movement of machine causing crush/entrapment
2. Machine overload or attachment failure causing crush or impact injury
3. Machinery damage
4. Struck by: boom, chassis, moving load
5. Ground unable to support machine
6. Load collides with structure
7. Load collides with other plant
8. Machine boom comes within arcing distance of overhead lines		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		Manufacturer's operator manual
Strategic Forum for Construction Plant Safety Group: Safe Use of Telehandlers in Construction
Working safely with agricultural machinery
AIS37 Operator seat restraints for mobile work equipment in agriculture and forestry
AIS8 Working safely near overhead electricity power lines
GS6 Avoiding danger from overhead power lines
  • All operators trained, authorised, medically fit and where appropriate certificated
  • PPE worn as a minimum: steel toe capped safety boots with good grip and ankle support, non-snag outer clothing
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • Unauthorised personnel prevented from entering exclusion zone (Barriers, tape and/or signs)
  • Clear work briefing provided with agreed work plan and communication method established
  • Machine only to be operated within its capabilities as designated by the manufacturer (safe lifting loads, and slope angles)
  • Site assessed for the presence of overhead lines, where found appropriate safe distances are to be applied
  • Maintenance conducted along with pre-use checks of machine in accordance with manufacturers handbook
  • All safety features and devices checked for presence and functionality prior to machine use
  • Where fitted seat belts must be worn
  • Operate machine outdoors only
  • Site levels should be appropriate to the machine/loads
  • Operators must work (when indicated) within safe load limits of machine being used and with any attachment correctly adjusted
  • Operators must operate at a suitable speed, without sudden changes of direction and aggressive use of controls and keep attachments low
  • Load-weight carried low – decrease rollover potential and increase the machine's stability while driving
  • When possible, do not drive over rough surfaces
  • Load pointed uphill when going up or down hills – rollover potential is decreased with proper use
  • Do not drive across slopes. Either drive directly up or down a slope or find another point of access for a steep slope
  • Drive around obstacles, never over
  • Never use the machine as an elevator or platform
  • Do not operate the machine away from the correct operator position
  • Ensure team are fully briefed on need to keep clear of load during lifting and during travelling
  • Do not place hands or feet inside moving components
144
Ladders and Stepladders↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Use of ladders, stepladders and tripod ladders1. Falls from ladder
2. Muscle injury from carrying, moving, erecting ladders, overreaching
3. Equipment failure
4. Slips
5. Electric shock		4416Operator
Work Team		HSE INDG455 Safe use of ladders and stepladders
AA Tree Work at Height ICoP
AA TG1: Tree climbing and aerial rescue
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Ladders/Stepladders to be thoroughly inspected annually and visually inspected prior to each occasion of use
  • Working at height regulations applied and follow the HSE guidelines GS 31 on safe use of ladders, steps and trestles
  • Ladder to be securely footed or tied during use; Stepladder to be opened fully and on even ground
  • No ladder use in high winds
  • Area below ladder to be cordoned off if in public area
  • Operators are not to lift heavy/awkward loads whilst on ladder/stepladder
  • Care should be taken when moving/carrying ladder or stepladder
  • Suitable sturdy footwear (PPE) to be worn with clean soles for grip
  • Use outdoors prohibited in icy wet conditions
  • Never work within 10m of live overhead power lines
  • Avoid/be aware of electrical appliances e.g. external lights
  • Any defects on ladders/steps found must be reported and recorded and equipment should be labelled appropriately
  • Ladder to be at a 75° angle on firm and level base and evenly supported on both stiles
  • To extend 1m above landing place for handhold and secured where possible
  • Where possible use 2 persons to move, lift and lower ladder to location and into position
  • Ladders to be positioned correct way up; Rungs to be kept free of mud and soles boots to be cleaned before use
  • Only one person to be on ladder at any time
  • A second person should be at the base of the ladder to add stability and to keep the area clear
  • PPE must include a helmet with chinstrap (EN12492)
  • Do not place ladder where it can be dislodged or struck by traffic
  • Do not use top platform unless it is designed with special handholds/rails
  • Only industrial grade stepladders are to be used. Step Ladders must be Class 1 (the highest rated in terms of strength and quality). These ladders are suitable for use in heavy duty industrial applications and environments. BS EN 131 ladders (previously "Class 2") may be used for light work
  • Criteria for safe use of leaning ladders: Duration of work to be less than 30 mins; Light work to be undertaken (Less than or equal to 10kg); Ground side slope does not exceed 16°; Ground back slope does not exceed 6°; Pre-use check completed, and item deemed safe for use; Ground found to be firm and level; Strong upper resting found and to be used; Ladder angle correct at 75° – 1 in 4 rule; Work can be completed without using the top 3 rungs; Work can be completed without overreaching
  • Criteria for safe use of stepladders: Duration of work to be less than 30 mins; Light work to be undertaken (Less than or equal to 10kg); Space available to fully open ladders; Any locking devices can be used properly; Pre-use check completed, and item deemed safe for use; Ground found to be firm and level; Side on working can be avoided; Work can be completed not using the top two steps
144
Tractor/Unimog↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Tractor Use1. Injury from sharp edges, swinging doors, movement in/out of vehicle; fuel contamination; explosion
2. Collisions and other accidents causing strains, sprains, cuts and bruising, possible muscular and skeletal damage, amputation to driver, passenger and pedestrian
3. Over turning – Loss of control causing crushing laceration, bodily injury to the driver and others. Muscular and skeletal damage, amputation to driver and pedestrians
4. Riding on vehicle – Falling off, serious injury
5. Falls from vehicles – Drivers may suffer serious, possibly fatal, injury should they fall from a vehicle
6. Contact with site features
7. Road traffic collision		4520Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		INDG185 Using tractors safely
INDG214 Working safely with agricultural machinery
AIS37 Operator seat restraints for mobile work equipment in agriculture and forestry
AIS8 Working safely near overhead electricity power lines
GS6 Avoiding danger from overhead power lines
  • All operators trained, authorised, medically fit and where appropriate certificated
  • PPE worn as a minimum: steel toe capped safety boots with good grip and ankle support, non-snag outer clothing
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • Safety features where fitted, function in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Safe working distances as stated within operator's manual maintained between operators on site and from the public
  • Work to stop if safe working zones entered by another person
  • Machinery maintained in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Warning signs placed at all reasonably foreseeable approaches to the worksite indicating hazard
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Pre-use tractor checks completed with defects reported to management
  • Keep work boots and vehicle steps clean
  • Fuel, tools and equipment are not to be carried in passenger compartments
  • Unauthorised passengers where additional seating is not fitted are not to be transported inside or on the tractor
  • Do not carry out maintenance or adjustments whilst the equipment is running
  • Do not carry out any maintenance or repairs if you have not been trained to do so
  • Always complete the required daily/weekly and periodic inspection documents
  • All drivers must hold the appropriate driving licence for the vehicle being driven
  • Always drive within the correct legal speed limits
  • Avoid driving tractor across slopes; Avoid using tractor on slopes greater than 15 degrees
  • Drivers are not to jump from cab or trailer
  • Exit tractor cab facing forward, not jumping from the last step
  • Do not place hands or feet in areas with movement components
  • Do not stand between vehicle and walls or implements
  • Drivers are to observe surroundings when reversing vehicle
  • Always ensure handbrake is applied before leaving the cab
  • Do not drive the machine at any person, if people approach within 5 meters STOP
  • Check work area prior to use
  • Ensure all road lights are working and effective when driving on the road
  • Ensure tractor is fitted with a flashing beacon that is visible to traffic in all directions to warn of slow-moving vehicle
  • If tractor is working on roadways, then ensure warning signs are erected to warn road users and suitable blue directional arrow is fitted to the unit to warn others of location
  • If approached or confronted with people or animals, switch off immediately
155
Telescopic Handler↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Telescopic Handler1. Struck by: Telehandler boom, Telehandler chassis, Moving load
2. Ground unable to support telehandler
3. Telehandler overloaded
4. Telehandler failure
5. Load collides with structure
6. Load collides with other plant – cranes, excavators etc.
7. Telehandler boom comes within arcing distance of overhead lines
8. Persons hand crushed/trapped by load
9. Load may fall on person
10. Loose parts on load may fall
11. Person falling from height when attaching or removing slings from load
12. High wind causes load to collide with fixed object
13. Telehandler becomes unstable		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		Strategic Forum for Construction Plant Safety Group: Safe Use of Telehandlers In Construction
SFPSG Guidance on the Safe Use of Quickhitches
Working safely with agricultural machinery
AIS37, AIS8
GS6
L117 ACOP for rider operated lift trucks
INDG413 Preventing falls from vehicles
