  • All operators trained, authorised, medically fit and where appropriate certificated
  • PPE worn as a minimum: steel toe capped safety boots with good grip and ankle support, non-snag outer clothing
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • Safety features where fitted, function in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Seat belt must be worn where fitted
  • Flashing beacons where fitted to be used during operation
  • Safe working distances as stated within operator's manual maintained between operators on site and from the public
  • Work to stop if safe working zones entered by another person
  • Machinery maintained in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Warning signs placed at all reasonably foreseeable approaches to the worksite indicating hazard
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Where fitted seatbelts must be worn
  • Ensure team are fully briefed on need to keep clear of load during lifting and telehandler during travelling
  • Establish presence of voids/underground services
  • Assess ground and establish if there is a requirement for stabilizer mats
  • Tag line to be attached to load to control rotation
  • If present arrange for isolation or position telehandler boom/load outside minimum safe approach distance
  • Ensure lifting accessories with adequate capacity have been selected, that they are adequately maintained, pre-use checks are carried out
  • Inspect load for loose objects prior to lift and secure/remove loose items
144
Excavator↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Excavator use1. Crush Injury
2. Property damage
3. Respiratory problems due to dust
4. Dermatitis due to oil and grease
5. Eye injuries
6. Burns and scalds
7. Electrocution
8. Noise-induced hearing loss		4520Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		Strategic Forum for Construction Plant Safety Group: Safe Use of Telehandlers in Construction
AIS8 Working safely near overhead electricity power lines
GS6 Avoiding danger from overhead power lines
CITB Health and Safety advice for Plant Operators
  • All operators trained, authorised, medically fit and where appropriate certificated
  • PPE worn as a minimum: steel toe capped safety boots with good grip and ankle support, non-snag outer clothing
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • Safety features where fitted, function in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Where fitted seat belts must be worn; Where fitted flashing beacons must be used during machine operation
  • Mirrors or CCTV to be in place on machine to allow for 360° vision for machine operator
  • Safe working distances as stated within operator's manual maintained
  • Work to stop if safe working zones entered by another person
  • Machinery maintained in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Warning signs placed at all reasonably foreseeable approaches to the worksite indicating hazard
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Where an excavator fitted with a quick hitch device is being operated, drivers should ensure the following before operating the machine: The correct procedure for securing the attachment (bucket/rock breaker etc.) is employed and that the locking mechanism/safety pin is fully deployed and secured. Once attachment is secured, that before use and when all persons in the vicinity are sufficiently clear of the machine, that the driver then aggressively shake the dipper arm to ensure attachment cannot come loose. Once step 2 is complete and work commences, never allow persons to work directly under the excavator arm attachment. If an excavator driver cannot secure the attachment to the quick hitch due to a missing safety pin, the driver must not operate the machine until his/her employer is informed and a safety pin has been sourced
  • Windows, lights, Mirrors to be cleaned regularly
  • The cab floor to be kept clean and free from any obstacles which may affect the safe operation of the Machine
  • Ground workers to wear Hi Visibility Vests and helmets always, where possible separate pedestrian and vehicular routes to be established
  • Ground workers to keep clear of moving Excavator always and not to approach until signalled to do so by the trained Operator
  • The Excavator to be equipped with a Fire extinguisher
  • Competent mechanics will carry out servicing to plant as per the Manufacturer's Instructions
  • The use of Mobile phones will be prohibited during machine operation
  • When mounting or dismounting the machine the handholds and steps must be used. These will be maintained in safe condition
  • Operators must not approach overhead power lines until adequate precautions are in place and are authorised to do so by site supervision
  • Driver must never operate under overhead lines without consultation with site management and appropriate control measures
  • When working in the vicinity of Overhead Cables, goal posts will be in position and warning signs erected
  • Height restrictors will be fitted to designated machines
  • Where underground services are known to be present survey the area (i.e. Scanner) and mark location of services prior to commencement of work. The location of services will be discussed with the operator to ensure that he/she understands the work task as laid out
  • Warning signs will be erected to alert pedestrians of Excavator operation
  • Operators not to leave the Operators Seat or Park the machine while on a slope
155
Hydraulic Loading Arm/Forwarder↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Hydraulic loading arm, Forwarding1. Equipment failure
2. Crush injuries from falling or swinging material
3. Working from hook/basket
4. Contact with power lines		4520Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		Manufacturer's operator manual
Lantra Awards L2/3 Competencies
CPA/ALLMI – Best Practice Guide – The Management of Lifting Operations with Lorry Loaders
  • Do not operate the loader if any part of the machine or its load can come within one tree length + the vicinity zone (down to a minimum distance of 15m) of energised overhead power lines
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated and conversant with tree work
  • All guards and warnings in place
  • Regular machine maintenance undertaken
  • Unauthorised personnel excluded from work area: signs, barriers, etc.
  • Team communication established and maintained
  • Agreed work plan between all operators
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance, safety footwear as a minimum, and head protection when working outside a FOPS protected cab
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Do not place hands or feet inside moving components
  • Site set up and operator communication should be undertaken to ensure no person walks beneath the loading arm or suspended loads
  • Loaders must be operated on firm level ground with where fitted the outriggers properly deployed and the vehicles tyres correctly inflated
  • If applicable spreader plates may be required under the outrigger feet
  • Machines may only be used with outriggers where fitted partially retracted if this duty is permitted by the manufacturer
  • Loaders should always be positioned so that there is no risk of the operator being trapped between the base unit and the load or loader arm
  • Operating controls should be clearly marked. Where more than one operating position is provided the operator should ensure that no person is able to interfere with the second set of controls
  • Operators should have a clear view of the whole lifting operation
  • Where the SWL is dependent upon the operating radius or boom configuration, a load radius diagram must be provided at each operating position
  • Avoid driving across felled trees or other timber
  • Where side slopes are unavoidable, extend the loader boom to the uphill side to maintain stability
  • Ensure the boom does not come into contact with any obstruction
  • Ensure the loading or parking brake is on while loading, and that it is released before moving
  • When loading/unloading on sloping ground, park straight up and down the slope
  • Use convenient stumps or ground obstructions to chock the wheels when loading on slopes
  • Use more than one control at a time to give smooth movement
  • When loading, ensure the grapple jaws fully encircle the load (unless this is a single billet)
  • Load the bunk evenly to maximise stability and do not overload it
  • Do not load above the level of the headboard and stanchions/pins
155
Mulcher↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Mulcher use1. Crush Injury
2. Property damage
3. Respiratory problems due to dust
4. Dermatitis due to oil and greases
5. Eye injuries
6. Burns and scalds
7. Electrocution
8. Noise-induced hearing loss		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA 204 Flails and mulchers in tree work
AIS8 Working safely near overhead electricity power lines
GS6 Avoiding danger from overhead power lines
CITB Health and Safety advice for Plant Operators
AS24 Power take-offs and power take-off drive shafts
  • All operators trained, authorised, medically fit and where appropriate certificated
  • PPE worn as a minimum: steel toe capped safety boots with good grip and ankle support, non-snag outer clothing
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • Safety features where fitted, function in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Where fitted seat belts must be worn; Where fitted flashing beacons must be used during machine operation
  • Safe working distances as stated within operator's manual maintained between operators on site, the machine and from the public
  • Work to stop if safe working zones entered by another person
  • Machinery maintained in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Warning signs placed at all reasonably foreseeable approaches to the worksite indicating hazard
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Base vehicles are to be fitted with an operator protective structure (OPS) designed to minimise the risk of injury from flying debris entering the cab – OPS may take the form of a framework with mesh grills, safety glazing or polycarbonate
  • Where OPS obscures visibility, 360° vision should be established through either mirrors or CCTV
  • Doors and windows are not to be removed from the base vehicle
  • Check that the weight of the mulcher does not exceed the maximum load for the front or rear axles and tyres of the base vehicle – use counterbalance weights or ballast where necessary
  • If the machine is powered by the base vehicle's hydraulic system, ensure the hydraulic capacity is adequate for the type of flail/mulcher being used
  • If the machine is powered by the base vehicle's power takeoff (PTO) shaft, ensure: the PTO speed is compatible with the flail/mulcher and the PTO shaft; the PTO shaft is fitted with a suitable guard (complying with BS EN ISO 5674) enclosing the shaft along its full length, from the base vehicle to the flail
  • Safety devices (e.g. shearbolts, pressure-relief valves, PTO torque clutches and belts) should be fitted in accordance with the manufacturer's handbook
  • Front- or rear-mounted mulchers must be fitted with cutting height adjustment and a pusher bar to prevent tall, woody material falling onto the base vehicle cab
  • A sign warning against working in the vicinity of overhead electric power lines should be prominently displayed (in all machines capable of exceeding 4.2m in height), together with the maximum height of the machine and the maximum height in the recommended travelling position
  • Do not leave the base vehicle cab until the cutting head has stopped rotating and has been lowered to the ground
  • Extend the risk zone or adjust working methods when operating on undulating ground
  • Reduce the risk of flying debris by: working at a slower speed; making two or more passes over a work area
  • Be aware of the risk of flying debris when "breaking out" into open spaces, e.g. roads, pathways, rides etc.
  • Stop work and disengage the PTO or drive to the machine immediately if any part of the flail/mulcher becomes loose or is damaged
  • Where possible, excavator-based flails/mulchers should be operated using a slewing motion, with the head at right angles to the operator's cab, so that debris is not directed towards cab windows or the vehicle. If it is necessary to operate the head in line with the cab then: cutting should always be away from the operator; an adjustable debris deflector plate must be fitted; a suitable cab operator protective structure must be fitted; and the head should not be operated above the level of the cab
144
Tree Shear and Grapple Saw↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Pre-use checks, and routine maintenance1. Fire/explosion
2. Burns/blast injuries
3. Injury through entrapment, entanglement, hot surfaces, contamination, cuts, crush
4. Fall from height
5. Dermatitis/Skin disorders
6. Debris in eyes
7. Injury through accidental starting of machinery		3412Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA 608 Tree Shear and Grapple Saw
  • Safe distance of at least 10m applied from any ignition source
  • Fuel caps replaced securely; Spill provision provided along with operator training given in the use of such systems
  • Spouts, funnels, and pumps to be utilised as appropriate
  • PPE to be worn in accordance with up to date company COSHH assessment (Petrol, grease, oil)
  • When re-fuelling the manufacturer's instructions and any instructions on starting plant/equipment/machinery must be followed
  • Always ensure that no members of the public or other staff are in the area when refuelling
  • Exhaust/engine guards to be in place
  • Through training the operator is made aware of the hot exhaust and engine
  • Machinery shall be allowed to cool prior to fuelling, greasing and oil replenishment
  • The fuel cap is never removed whilst the engine is running
  • If fuel is spilled on clothing, clothing is changed immediately
  • Fire extinguisher serviced and available
  • Ensure safe stop procedures are in place prior to any maintenance activity being undertaken
  • Allow components to become stationary and cool down
  • No unauthorised modifications to equipment or machinery to be undertaken
  • Any repairs, adjustments etc. must only be carried out by a competent person
  • Do not wear loose clothing and ensure long hair is tied back
  • Wear safety helmet to EN397 or EN12492 when working below machine parts
  • Wear safety footwear with good grip
  • Wear gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1), considering grip, cut resistant, absorption
  • Manufacturers handbook to be made available and consulted
  • No individual to climb upon the machinery structure
  • Use of designated access steps only when access required
  • Work is only carried out in good light and weather conditions
144
Machine being driven/movement and parking1. Failure to ensure the machine is serviceable may lead to collision
2. Adverse weather can increase likelihood of collisions and/or loss of machine control
3. Struck by/collision with a third party		3412Operator
Work Team
Public
Client
  • All exterior lights checked; Fuel, and oil levels checked prior to use
  • Tyres checked for condition and pressure; All windows and mirrors to be clear and vision unobscured
  • If severe weather is forecast, journey should be delayed or cancelled
  • Vehicles to be maintained as part of ongoing company maintenance procedures
  • Manner of driving adjusted to account for the adverse weather conditions
  • Procedures shall be in place in the event of machinery breakdown on route to site
  • Drivers instructed not to reverse, unless necessary
  • Exclude pedestrians from areas where machinery movements are to take place
  • Where reasonably practicable fit CCTV, convex mirrors, Fresnel lens etc. to overcome restrictions to visibility from the driver's seat
  • Ensure everyone on site understands site rules on vehicle safety
  • Drivers and signallers need to be in constant communication during reversing operations
  • Signallers should not be put at risk from vehicle movements, e.g. by standing directly behind reversing vehicles
  • Ensure reversing warning lights and alarms are in good working order and instruct workers to keep clear of moving vehicles
  • Ensure only trained, authorised and where applicable certificated individuals undertake signaller tasks
144
People in the immediate area1. Struck by: (a) Loader boom; (b) Loader chassis; (c) Moving load4416Operator
Work Team
Public
  • The machine operator is to be trained, authorised, medically fit, and where appropriate certificated in the use of the machinery and its attachments
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • The machine operator must maintain visual awareness throughout the task
  • Communication links established between all parties on site prior to commencing works, between the machine operator and groundstaff
  • Safety features where fitted, are present and function in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Flashing beacons where fitted to be used during operation
  • Safe working distances as stated within the operator's manual maintained between operators on site and from the public
  • Work to stop if safe working zones entered by another person
  • Warning signs placed at all reasonably foreseeable approaches to the worksite indicating hazard
  • Ensure the work team are fully briefed on the need to keep clear of any load and the machine whilst in motion
144
Machine stability1. Ground unable to support machine
2. Machine overloaded
3. Machine and/or attachment failure
4. Machinery use on sloping ground		4416Operator
  • Prepare site – walk site prior to starting work to identify significant hazards
  • Establish presence of voids/underground services
  • Assess ground and establish if there is a requirement for stabilizer mats
  • Operator to confirm any mats supplied are suitable for the machine type and model
  • Operator to examine ground surface prior to lifting operation to ensure no obvious signs of soft ground or surface erosion
  • Ensure lifting attachments with adequate capacity have been selected, that they are adequately maintained, pre-use checks are carried out
  • Ensure the machine has been adequately maintained and has current report of thorough examination
  • Machine set up in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Operator to undertake visual inspection of vehicle and boom to ensure no obvious signs of a defect prior to any lift
  • Stabilisers must be fully extended
  • Operators must be familiar with the load charts for the equipment in use
  • Accurately assess the maximum radius at which the load can be lifted
  • When travelling with load, keep the load as low as reasonably practicable
  • A simple lifting plan/method statement or similar shall be developed to include type of equipment in use, loads being lifting, pick-up/delivery points, ground/operational areas, exclusion zones, traffic routes
  • Company standard operating procedures are to be followed to ensure the safe use of the machine
  • Do not use machinery on slopes/gradients over the manufacturers recommended limits
144
Movement of load1. Load collides with another structure
2. Load collides with other plant
3. Boom comes within arcing distance of overhead lines (OHPL)		4416Operator
  • Operator is to visually inspect the load for broken/hanging material and adjust the work technique to account for this
  • If present arrange for isolation of the OHPL or position machinery boom/load outside minimum safe working distance
  • No material is to come within 10m of an OHPL without prior consultation with the Regional Electricity Company supplier (REC)
  • The use of barriers/barricades shall be used to clearly indicate "no go" areas for machinery use
  • Emergency procedures recorded and communicated
  • Undertaking of visual site inspection prior to starting work to identify presence of UG or OH infrastructure
  • Means of contacting REC/Distribution Network Operator is known to all staff
  • Move slowly and cautiously when the load is raised and/or visibility hampered by load
  • Where machinery is to pass under OHPLs, goalposts shall be used to ensure a safe passage can be maintained
144
Suspended load1. Load may fall on person
2. Load may break up and fall		4416Operator
  • Ensure the machine has been adequately maintained, has current report of thorough examination and that pre-use checks are carried out
  • Ensure attachments with adequate capacity have been selected, that they are adequately maintained, have current report of thorough examination and that pre-use checks are carried out
  • Site personnel not involved in the lifting operation should be controlled so that they do not encroach into the lifting radius
  • Inspect load prior to lift for insecure branches, broken tops (remove these first)
  • All personnel to wear hard hats
  • The drop zone is the area within which it is anticipated material may fall – this must always be kept clear. Drop zones should also include where material is to be placed down, and the routes between these areas
  • Never lift loads over persons
  • No individual is to be ever positioned directly beneath a load that is suspended or being lifted
144
Use of cutting head attachment1. Injuries from moving parts
2. Crush/trap injuries
3. Cuts, bruises, and contamination
4. Damage to plant
5. Struck by – Flying debris
6. Chain shot		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Pre-use checks to be completed in accordance with manufacturer's instructions
  • Only timber shall be cut with the cutting attachment
  • Material should be processed from a safe and stable position
  • Material to be processed must be within the operating tolerances of the cutting head as stated by the manufacturer
  • Operators are to have access to both the cutting head and base unit operator's manual
  • No alterations or modifications are to be made to the cutting head without consent from the manufacturer
  • Material to be processed should be in a near to as upright position as possible
  • Material for processing must not hang over the operator's position
  • Processed material should be positioned where the base machine can safely reach
  • Material only to be felled away from or parallel to any overhead structure
  • A minimum of a 1m buffer zone should be maintained between the base unit and obstructions e.g. Fences
  • Operator to switch off machine after use
  • Do not place hands or feet inside moving components
  • Bystanders to maintain safety distances as specified by the manufacturer
  • Chain sharpening and bar maintenance carried out in accordance with manufacturers specification, including visual pre use checks
  • The operator shall conduct a test run of all functions prior as part of the starting procedure
  • Material to be cut in short sections, ensure no foreign material enters the saw unit
  • Observe the wind speed prior to cutting, wind speed that will have a direct affect over the control of what has been cut should result in work stopping
  • Regular bar maintenance shall be carried out to ensure oilways are clear, rails are straight and correct depth maintained
  • Sprocket wear is not to exceed manufacturers recommendations (approx. 0.6mm)
  • Pre use checks of the cutting head by the operator shall include a visual inspection of the chain for defects and to confirm it is safe for use
  • Ensure as part of pre use checks chain tension is correct
  • The cutting head is never to be pointed at people whilst running
  • The saw head shall be engaged as close to the timber being processed as possible
144
Flail↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Use of a tractor mounted flail1. Injury from moving parts
2. Crush/trap injuries
3. Cuts and contamination
4. Fire/explosion
5. Third parties struck by flail or tractor
6. Overturn or loss of machinery control
7. Ejected debris
8. Noise
9. Dust
10. Operator injury – back strain, slips/trips
11. Maintenance injuries – contamination, high fluids under pressure
12. Electrocution
13. Property damage		4416OperatorHSE AIS21 Safe use of rotary flail hedge cutters
FISA 204 Flails and mulchers in tree work
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Regular machine maintenance ensuring all safety features present and working (no hot or rotating parts exposed)
  • Do not enter body parts into any danger zone such as rotor shafts, flail compartments, linkages unless the machinery is completely shut down
  • Unauthorised personnel excluded from work area: signs, barriers, etc.
  • Flail not to be operated when any third party enters the machines risk zone
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Operator to be protected inside machine cab with guarded or penetrating resistant windows
  • Windows to be kept clean to allow good visibility
  • Where works affect the highway Chapter 8 DoT requirements to be deployed
  • Hazard beacons, present, clean, working and used
  • Material to be flailed inspected prior to works to ensure hazard material or items are removed or very clearly marked so to be avoided
  • Side windows of cab always to be closed during flail use
  • Never attempt to clear jammed material from the flail, without the engine being stopped and the flail having stop rotated
  • Flail head designed to direct flying debris away from cab
  • Control levers to automatically return to neutral (either when released or when power disengaged depending on operational needs)
  • Ballast/counterweight added to vehicle to ensure the machine remains stable when attachment is in use – ensure vehicle is the correct weight for using the flail
  • Ensure any attachment removed from a vehicle is left stable using any stand or prop required
  • Work at a safe speed considering the terrain and any obstacles
  • No work must be carried out within 10m of an OHPL unless a system of work has been introduced that will prevent a flail arm contacting the line
144
Use of a flail – deck attachment, remote controlled1. Injury through road traffic collision
2. Flying debris injuries to operator and third parties
3. Property damage
4. Machinery damage – contact with foreign objects/entanglement
5. Machine runaway
6. Machine overturn
7. Loss of machine control
8. Electrocution/contact with underground services
9. Struck by the machine
10. Injuries from moving parts
11. Crush/trap injuries
12. Cuts, bruises and contamination
13. Fire/explosion		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Regular machine maintenance ensuring all safety features present and working (no hot or rotating parts exposed)
  • Do not enter body parts into any danger zone such as rotor shafts, flail compartments, linkages unless the machinery is completely shut down
  • Unauthorised personnel excluded from work area: signs, barriers, etc.
  • Flail not to be operated when any third party enters the machines risk zone
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Where works affect the highway Chapter 8 DoT requirements to be deployed
  • Hazard beacons, present, clean, working and used
  • Material to be flailed inspected prior to works to ensure hazard material or items are removed or very clearly marked so to be avoided
  • Never attempt to clear jammed material from the flail, without the engine being stopped and the flail having stop rotated
  • Remote controlled machine when flailing shall never be driven towards the operator
  • Control levers to automatically return to neutral (either when released or when power disengaged depending on operational needs)
  • Ensure any attachment removed from a vehicle is left stable using any stand or prop required
  • Work at a safe speed considering the terrain and any obstacles
  • No smoking/naked flames; All ignition sources shall be excluded from the maintenance area
  • Approved fuel storage containers shall be used; Storage of fuel shall be out of direct sunlight
  • Spill kits shall be provided and used where appropriate
  • The maximum degree of slope that the machine may work as specified by the manufacturer shall not be exceeded
  • Radio control boxes shall operate on a "dead-man's handle" basis ensuring that the machine stops if the operator lets go of the controls or if the battery fails
  • Audible warnings shall be emitted if the machine is operated close to the range of the radio control signal
  • The machines operator must always remain in visual contact with the machine
  • The machine shall not be tracked around site with the flail head in the elevated position
  • A safe working distance of at least 5m between remote controlled machinery and the operator shall be maintained
144
Air Spade↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Use of an Air Spade1. Manual handling
2. Contact with sub surface features and services
3. Use of tools
4. Use of compressed air
5. Chemical and biotic factors: discarded clinical sharps (needles); Animal waste; Contact with soil; Contact with previously used and unidentified chemical products
6. Vibration: Work related upper limb disorder; Vibration white finger		4416Operator
Work Team
General Public
Client
  • All operators trained, authorised, medically fit and where appropriate certificated
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • Safety features where fitted, function in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Safe working distances as stated within operator's manual maintained between operators on site and from the public
  • Work to stop if safe working zones entered by another person
  • Machinery maintained in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Warning signs placed at all reasonably foreseeable approaches to the worksite indicating hazard
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Pre-use checks completed with defects reported to management
  • Do not carry out any maintenance or repairs if you have not been trained to do so
  • All operators shall be briefed upon the manual handling risk assessment
  • All operators shall have received instruction from a competent person in the handling of the type and shape of load to be encountered
  • Manual handling shall be avoided as much as possible. Mechanised resources shall be utilised wherever possible
  • A first aid kit must be available on site
  • The air spade unit may weigh in excess of an operator's personal capacity and so assistance must be used when coupling the unit to a towing vehicle
  • Prior to breaking the surface with any tool: (i) The services plan shall be sourced and examined for the location of buried services; (ii) The area shall be scanned using a recently calibrated cat scanner operated by a competent person; (iii) A permit to dig shall be obtained where appropriate
  • Eye and face protection shall be worn when working with compressed air
  • A dust mask shall be used
  • Hearing protection shall be worn when using any tool which produced loud noise in use (80dba or above)
  • Gloves shall be worn when using the air spade
  • A disposable coverall may be used when conditions are particularly dirty
  • The air spade shall be run at a rate of 185 cfm
  • Hoses shall be attached using a whip check mechanism to prevent fling in the event of attachment failure
  • The air spade blast shall be set at 150mph
  • Sites shall be delineated using signs, cones and barrier tape as appropriate
  • Hand saws shall be used to prune roots rather than chainsaws to avoid contact with soil and stones
  • Excavations shall be filled or covered prior to leaving site
  • All staff shall be briefed on the procedure in relation to each of the identified hazards: (i) Low growing ground covering vegetation shall be cleared using tools in order to expose the presence of discarded needles; (ii) If needles are found, the client shall be informed, and arrangements made for a specialist to clear the area; (iii) Soiling shall be washed from hands prior to eating or using the toilet; (iv) Gloves shall be worn where reasonably practicable; (v) Where chemical contamination is suspected, specialist advice shall be sought; (vi) Animal waste shall be marked and avoided or moved if practicable; (vii) Even minor cuts and abrasions shall be thoroughly cleaned and dressed to avoid soil borne infections
144
Mowing↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Flail or swipe mowing1. Contact with moving parts when clearing blockages
2. Being trapped or crushed while moving the mower between the work and transport positions
3. Being crushed below a raised machine while replacing blades or carrying out other maintenance work
4. Being struck or run over by the tractor and mower
5. Overturns when working on slopes or banks
6. Being struck by objects or debris ejected from the machine		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA 802, 804, 805, 806
INDG362, INDG363
AIS25 Safe use of agricultural mowers
INDG143 Manual handling at work
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • PPE worn as a minimum: steel toe capped safety boots with good grip and ankle support, non-snag outer clothing
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • Operators should walk the site initially to identify any hazards that need to be clearly marked or removed
  • Ensure safety features where fitted and function in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Safe working distances as stated within operator's manual are to be maintained between operators on site and from the public
  • Do not operate a mower if there are bystanders who are likely to be hit by ejected debris (these may travel considerable distances)
  • Stop work if you see someone approaching you when mowing is taking place
  • Take care when working on steep ground, particularly when turning, and especially with mounted mowers
  • Work to stop if safe working zones entered by another person
  • Make sure all guards are in position, correctly fitted and secure before starting work. Do not continue work with the guards missing or damaged
  • Make sure that the protective skirt or other device to stop objects or debris from being ejected is in place and in good condition
  • Machinery shall be maintained in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Avoid manual handling where possible
  • Warning signs placed at all reasonably foreseeable approaches to the worksite indicating hazard
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Machinery driven/operated in accordance with operator's manual
  • Operators not to dismount a machine unless the motor is off, blades have stopped, and brake is set
  • Operators are not to jump from a cab; Mowers are not to be used for towing
  • Do not place hands or feet inside moving components
  • The operators driving including speed, should reflect the terrain being mowed, their own experience and the capability of the machinery
133
Pedestrian Mower Use1. Work Equipment – Injury from Entanglement, Entrapment, Impalement, Impact
2. Mower Overturning, Uncontrolled movement
3. Contact with third parties, people or animals
4. Ejected material
5. Contact with hazardous substances
6. Noise induced hearing loss
7. Hand Arm Vibration
8. Burns from hot engine parts or exhaust
9. Muscular Skeletal injury from using Work Equipment
10. Slips, trips and falls
11. Fire and explosion when refuelling Mower		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA 802, 804, 805, 806
INDG362, INDG363
AIS25 Safe use of agricultural mowers
INDG143
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • PPE worn as a minimum: steel toe capped safety boots with good grip and ankle support, non-snag outer clothing
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • Operators should walk the site initially to identify any hazards that need to be clearly marked or removed
  • Ensure safety features where fitted and function in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Safe working distances as stated within operator's manual are to be maintained between operators on site and from the public
  • Do not operate a mower if there are bystanders who are likely to be hit by ejected debris (these may travel considerable distances)
  • Stop work if you see someone approaching you when mowing is taking place
  • Take care when working on steep ground, particularly when turning, and especially with mounted mowers
  • Work to stop if safe working zones entered by another person
  • Make sure all guards are in position, correctly fitted and secure before starting work. Do not continue work with the guards missing or damaged
  • Mower must be suitable for the task and used within the design parameters of the equipment
  • Make sure that the protective skirt or other device to stop objects or debris from being ejected is in place and in good condition
  • Machinery shall be maintained in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Avoid manual handling where possible
  • Warning signs placed at all reasonably foreseeable approaches to the worksite indicating hazard
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Machinery operated in accordance with operator's manual; Mowers are not to be used for towing
  • Do not place hands or feet inside moving components
  • Wash mower blades and underside of equipment prior to maintenance
  • If possible do not run over dog faeces; Wear protective gloves if adjusting blades or areas where the faeces could have been splattered
  • Use anti-bacterial gel to decontaminate hands on completion of any work to mower
  • Only take to site quantity of fuel needed for the task; Use a non-spill container to transport the fuel
  • Refuel if possible, on hard concrete surface; Use a funnel when pouring; Decant from container slowly; Do not overfill fuel tank on the mower
  • Use rags to mop up any spillages; Ensure a spillage kit is available for the total amount of fuel in case of major spillage
  • Modern machinery shall be used which has the lowest practicable noise output rating
  • Machinery shall be rigorously maintained ensuring that noise characteristics are not exacerbated
  • Hearing protection shall be provided by the company and worn by the operator
  • Machinery selected for use shall be fitted with anti-vibration systems
  • Work shall be planned so that vibration exposure beyond the exposure limit value (ELV) is eliminated
  • Machinery shall be rigorously maintained ensuring positive function of braking systems
  • Machine secured from unauthorised use when not in use
  • All approved machinery shall have a 'dead man's handle'
  • Mowing shall be undertaken alongside roads rather than towards roads where reasonably practicable to do so
144
Use of a Ride on Mower1. Contact with moving parts when clearing blockages
2. Being trapped or crushed while moving the mower between the work and transport positions
3. Being crushed below a raised machine while replacing blades or carrying out other maintenance work
4. Being struck or run over by the mower
5. Overturns when working on slopes or banks
6. Being struck by objects or debris ejected from the machine		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA 802, 804, 805, 806
INDG362, INDG363
AIS25
INDG143
  • The unit shall be maintained in strict accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations
  • The unit shall be maintained by a qualified technician
  • All operators shall receive training prior to undertaking maintenance operations
  • Reference shall be made to the manufacturer's maintenance manual
  • PPE shall be provided and used where appropriate
  • Operators shall have received appropriate training from a competent person
  • Persons on site shall maintain a safe distance from the working machine as advised by the operator
  • Persons on site shall wear sufficient PPE as identified by the site/task specific risk assessment completed prior to commencing work on the day
  • All moving and hot parts shall be appropriately guarded
  • The operator shall not touch the blades
  • The operator shall remain seated in the driving seat whilst the blades are under power and shall not lift any guards whilst the blades are moving
  • Pre-start checks shall be undertaken in line with the maintenance and inspection guidance from the manufacturer
  • Controls shall be kept clear of debris and mud
  • Operator shall adhere strictly to the work safe procedure
  • Operators must not wear loose fitting clothing when operating this machinery
  • No smoking during machine use or fuelling to be enforced
  • All ignition sources shall be excluded from the maintenance and work area
  • Approved fuel storage containers shall be used; Storage of fuel is to be out of direct sunlight
  • Spill kits shall be provided, and emergency spillage procedures known to operators
  • Machinery shall be rigorously maintained
  • Site assessments/ongoing operational assessments
  • Parking shall be on level ground where possible
  • Keys shall be removed, and the cab locked when the machine is not in use
144
Rotavators↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Rotavator1. Loose clothing getting entangled in moving parts causing bodily damage to the operator
2. Entrapment and entanglement causing laceration, bodily injury to the operator and others. Muscular and skeletal damage, amputation to the operator
3. Contact with blade spikes causing amputation, severe cuts and laceration
4. Propelled objects and debris causing eye, body and skin damage, to employees and others. Damage to property		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
Client
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • PPE worn as a minimum: steel toe capped safety boots with good grip and ankle support, non-snag outer clothing
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • Safety features where fitted, function in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Employees must be aware that the motion of the blades and spikes may pull the equipment or cause it to "buck" if the blades or spikes hit an obstruction, employees must be prepared for this likelihood
  • Safe working distances as stated within operator's manual maintained between operators on site and from the public
  • Work to stop if safe working zones entered by another person
  • Machinery maintained in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Avoid manual handling where possible
  • Use of ergonomic work systems
  • Work planning considered
  • Warning signs placed at all reasonably foreseeable approaches to the worksite indicating hazard
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Do not place hands or feet inside moving components
133
Machine Operated Auger↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Use of machine operated augers1. Muscle injury from carrying, moving and fitting the auger
2. Equipment failure/damage
3. Flying debris
4. Contact with underground services		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
Client		FISA 607 – Firewood processor
INDG241 Farm Machinery Safety Guide
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Before using ensure there are no underground services at the bore location, this should include the use of CAT and GENNY devices
  • All reasonably foreseeable approaches to the work area are to be signed warning of work in progress
  • Use of guards/netting to reduce risk of flying debris e.g. stumpgrinder guard
  • Where fitted ensure the auger locking pin is installed correctly
  • Ensure the auger is correctly fitted to any machine in accordance with manufacturer's instructions
  • Ensure any fail to safe system e.g. shear bolts/pins are correctly located and as per manufacturer's instructions
  • Machine not to be left unattended especially when still running
  • Regularly clear soil from auger bit to prevent jamming – Safe Stop procedures MUST be used prior to removing debris
  • Operator to switch off machine after use
  • Safety glasses, safety footwear to be worn
  • No person is to go near auger bit when machine is operating – adopt a 5m safe working distance with the exception of the machine operator
  • Remove obstacles from operating area prior to boring
  • Operators to remain alert and always be ready to stop the engine straightaway if you feel the auger start to jam
  • If the machine starts to labour and slow down do not force it. Lift the machine with the auger, turning to allow soil, clay and stones to be freed from the hole. Steady drilling, keeping the auger speed up, is more effective than aggressive pressure
  • If you think the auger is becoming blunt, do not force the machine
  • If the auger jams in the ground do not continue boring – reverse the cycle to remove the auger
  • Manufacturers instruction manual to be available to operators
  • No modifications or alterations to the machine or auger are to be carried out without the manufacturers approval
  • Repairs and maintenance should only be carried out in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions (this may include teeth and pilot head replacement)
  • Fitting of the auger should be undertaken by at least two people
  • Operators must only lift items within their own personal capacity
  • Auger attachment must only be fitted to approved machine/base unit
  • Do not place hands or feet inside moving components
133
Tree Spade↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Use of a tree spade1. Electrocution, gas explosion – contact with underground/overhead services
2. Musculoskeletal injuries from manual handling
3. Crush injuries – heavy objects
4. Contact with substances hazardous to health
5. Slips and trips, uneven ground
6. Machine/attachment failure
7. Strike of operator by machine/product
8. Workplace transport injury e.g. knocked over		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
Client
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • All reasonably foreseeable approaches to the work area are to be signed warning of work in progress
  • Pre use checks to be completed in accordance with manufacturer's instructions
  • If applicable coupling and de-coupling of attachment undertaken in accordance with manufacturers guidance and only by authorised and competent operators
  • Material to be processed must be within the operating tolerances of the shear as stated by the manufacturer
  • Operators are to have access to both the tree spade and base unit operator's manual
  • No alterations or modifications are to be made to the tree spade without consent from the manufacturer
  • Spade to be CE marked
  • No material is to come within 10m of an OHPL without prior consultation with the Network Operator
  • The use of "dial before you dig", survey maps, CAT and GENNY and trail holes should be used to confirm the presence of underground services
  • Relevant COSHH assessments available at point of work
  • Eliminate manual handling through the use of mechanical aids; Operators trained in safe lifting techniques
  • PPE to include safety footwear, gloves and hi-visibility clothing for anyone working in conjunction with the machine
  • Machine to be fitted with mirrors to allow driver a 360° view
  • When in operation flashing beacons should be used
  • Where practicable audible reversing alarms should be fitted/operator sounds horn prior to reversing
  • Communication method between operators established prior to starting work
  • Do not place hands or feet inside moving components
133
Cone Splitter↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Use of a cone splitter1. Crush injury
2. High pressure fluid injection; Flying debris		4312Operator
Work Team
Public		INDG241 Farm Machinery Safety Guide
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Regular machine maintenance ensuring all safety features working
  • Unauthorised personnel excluded from work area: signs, barriers, etc. (min 2 x boom length or longest product being processed whichever is the greater)
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with industry guidance
  • Manufacturers instruction/user manual made available to all operators
  • Operator to be protected inside machine cab with guarded or penetrating resistant windows
  • Windows to be kept clean to allow good visibility
  • When practical and if fitted, use of machine dozer blade as a headboard when processing material
133
Merlo ROTO↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Pre-use checks, and routine maintenance1. Fire/explosion
2. Burns/blast injuries
3. Injury through entrapment, entanglement, hot surfaces, contamination, cuts, crush
4. Fall from height
5. Dermatitis/Skin disorders
6. Debris in eyes
7. Injury through accidental starting of machinery		4312Operator
Work Team
  • Safe distance of at least 10m applied from any ignition source
  • Fuel caps replaced securely; Spill provision provided along with operator training given in the use of such systems
  • Spouts, funnels, and pumps to be utilised as appropriate
  • PPE to be worn in accordance with up to date company COSHH assessment (Petrol, grease, oil)
  • When re-fuelling the manufacturer's instructions and any instructions on starting plant/equipment/machinery must be followed
  • Always ensure that no members of the public or other staff are in the area when refuelling
  • Exhaust/engine guards to be in place
  • Through training the operator is made aware of the hot exhaust and engine
  • Machinery shall be allowed to cool prior to fuelling, greasing and oil replenishment
  • The fuel cap is never removed whilst the engine is running
  • If fuel is spilled on clothing, clothing is changed immediately
  • Fire extinguisher serviced and available
  • Ensure safe stop procedures are in place prior to any maintenance activity being undertaken
  • Allow components to become stationary and cool down
  • No unauthorised modifications to equipment or machinery to be undertaken
  • Any repairs, adjustments etc. must only be carried out by a competent person
  • Do not wear loose clothing and ensure long hair is tied back
  • Wear safety helmet to EN397 or EN12492 when working below machine parts
  • Wear safety footwear with good grip
  • Wear gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1), considering grip, cut resistant, absorption
  • Manufacturers handbook to be made available and consulted
  • No individual to climb upon unauthorised areas of the machinery structure
  • Use of designated access steps only when access required
  • Work is only carried out in good light and weather conditions
133
Machine being driven/movement and parking1. Failure to ensure the machine is serviceable may lead to collision
2. Adverse weather can increase likelihood of collisions and/or loss of machine control
3. Struck by/collision with a third party		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
  • All exterior lights checked; Fuel, and oil levels checked prior to use
  • Tyres checked for condition and pressure; All windows and mirrors to be clear and vision unobscured
  • If severe weather is forecast, journey should be delayed or cancelled
  • Vehicles to be maintained as part of ongoing company maintenance procedures
  • Manner of driving adjusted to account for the adverse weather conditions which may include reduction of speed and an increase in distance from vehicles in front
  • Procedures shall be in place in the event of machinery breakdown on route to site/between sites
  • Drivers instructed not to reverse, unless necessary
  • Exclude pedestrians from areas where machinery movements are to take place. This should be using fixed barriers/plastic netting
  • Where reasonably practicable fit CCTV, convex mirrors, Fresnel lens etc. to overcome restrictions to visibility from the driver's seat
  • Ensure everyone on site understands site rules on vehicle safety
  • Drivers and signallers need to be in constant communication during reversing operations
  • Signallers should not be put at risk from vehicle movements, e.g. by standing directly behind reversing vehicles
  • Ensure reversing warning lights and alarms are in good working order and instruct workers to keep clear of moving vehicles
  • Ensure only trained, authorised and where applicable certificated individuals undertake signaller tasks
133
People in the immediate area1. Struck by: (a) Telehandler boom; (b) Telehandler chassis; (c) Moving load3412Operator
Work Team
Public
  • The machine operator is to be trained, authorised, medically fit, and where appropriate certificated in the use of the machinery and its attachments
  • Such work is not carried out while under the influence of alcohol or drugs or whilst feeling unwell
  • The machine operator must maintain visual awareness throughout the task
  • Communication links established between all parties on site prior to commencing works, in particular between the machine operator and groundstaff
  • Safety features where fitted, are present and function in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Seat belt must be worn where fitted; Flashing beacons where fitted to be used during operation
  • Safe working distances as stated within the operator's manual maintained between operators on site and from the public
  • Areas within which material may fall, particularly breaking up during movement should where practical be cordoned off using plastic barrier fencing
  • Work to stop if safe working zones entered by another person
  • Warning signs placed at all reasonably foreseeable approaches to the worksite indicating hazard
  • Ensure the work team are fully briefed on the need to keep clear of any load and the machine whilst in motion
144
Machine stability1. Ground unable to support machine
2. Machine overloaded
3. Machine and/or attachment failure
4. Machinery use on sloping ground		3412Operator
Work Team
Public
  • Prepare site – walk site prior to starting work to identify significant hazards
  • Establish presence of voids/underground services
  • Assess ground and establish if there is a requirement for stabiliser mats
  • Operator to confirm any mats supplied are suitable for the machine type and model
  • Operator to examine ground surface prior to lifting operation to ensure no obvious signs of soft ground or surface erosion
  • Ensure lifting attachments with adequate capacity have been selected, that they are adequately maintained, pre-use checks are carried out
  • Ensure the machine has been adequately maintained and has current report of thorough examination
  • Machine set up in accordance with manufacturers guidance
  • Operator to undertake visual inspection of vehicle and boom to ensure no obvious signs of a defect prior to any lift
  • Stabilisers must be fully extended
  • Operators must be familiar with the load charts for the equipment in use
  • Accurately assess the maximum radius at which the load can be lifted
  • When travelling with load, keep the load as low as reasonably practicable
  • A simple lifting plan/method statement or similar shall be developed to include type of equipment in use, loads being lifting, pick-up/delivery points, ground/operational areas, exclusion zones, traffic routes
  • Company standard operating procedures are to be followed to ensure the safe use of the machine
  • Do not use machinery on slopes/gradients over the manufacturers recommended limits
  • Where fitted seat belts are always to be worn
  • Using machinery across the face of a slope to be avoided unless machinery is specifically designed for this purpose
  • Brake operation, where appropriate, has been checked frequently and brakes are regularly serviced as required
  • Starting, stopping, or turning of machinery is avoided on a slope
  • Care is taken to watch for hidden holes or soft ground following heavy rain
  • Passengers are never carried on machinery
144
Movement of load1. Load collides with another structure
2. Load collides with other plant
3. Load collides with remaining tree canopy
4. Boom comes within arcing distance of overhead lines (OHPL)		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
  • No lone working allowed
  • Emergency procedures recorded and communicated
  • Undertaking of visual site inspection prior to starting work to identify presence of UG or OH infrastructure
  • Means of contacting Regional Electricity Company/Distribution Network Operator is known to all staff
  • If present arrange for isolation of the OHPL or position machinery boom/load outside minimum safe working distance
  • No material is to come within 10m of an OHPL without prior consultation with the Regional Electricity Company supplier (REC)
  • The use of barriers/barricades shall be used to clearly indicate "no go" areas for machinery use
  • Where machinery is to pass under OHPLs, goalposts shall be used to ensure a safe passage can be maintained
  • Operator is to visually inspect the load for broken/hanging material and adjust the work technique to account for this
  • Move slowly and cautiously when the load is raised and/or visibility hampered by load
  • Avoid sharp turns and heavy braking and ensure tyres are correctly inflated
133
Suspended load1. Load may fall on person
2. Load may break up and fall		4312Operator
Work Team
Public
  • Ensure the machine has been adequately maintained, has current report of thorough examination and that pre-use checks are carried out
  • Ensure attachments with adequate capacity have been selected, that they are adequately maintained, have current report of thorough examination and that pre-use checks are carried out
  • Site personnel not involved in the lifting operation should be controlled so that they do not encroach into the lifting radius
  • Inspect load prior to lift for insecure branches, broken tops (remove these first)
  • All personnel to wear hard hats
  • The drop zone is the area within which it is anticipated material may fall – this must always be kept clear. Drop zones should also include where material is to be placed down, and the routes between these areas
  • Never lift loads over persons
  • No individual is to be ever positioned directly beneath a load that is suspended or being lifted
  • The machine shall be fitted with a falling objects protective structure (FOPS)
133
Changing of attachments – general1. Unexpected movement of equipment causing crush or entrapment
2. Musculo-skeletal injury from lifting or adjusting the attachment
3. Contamination from grease
4. Failure of attachment through incorrect fitting		4312Operator
Work Team
  • Ensure machine and attachment manufacturers handbook is available
  • Ensure hitch systems are compatible
  • Attachments to be used must be authorised by the manufacturer for the machine type
  • Ensure when used operator and third-party communication is clear and hand signals established
  • Appropriate PPE to include safety footwear, non-snag outer clothing, gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1)
  • No work to take place beneath unsupported components
  • Ensure attachment and machine are stationary and stable (attachment fitting only on level ground)
  • Operational controls to be operated only from manufacturers designated working positions e.g. on operators' seat, as opposed to leaning inside cab
  • Operator to ensure attachment is correctly attached before moving away ensuring where fitted all pins have lynch pins, pick up hooks correctly engaged, use of "shake, rattle and roll" where appropriate
  • Maintenance conducted on attachments as per manufacturers guidance by competent and authorised individuals
  • Do not fit damaged attachments, defect reporting procedures to be in place
133
Changing of attachments – connection of hydraulics & electrics1. High pressure hydraulic injection
2. Struck by coupling
3. Personal contamination
4. Burns from electric connection
5. High pressure air injection		4312Operator
Work Team
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Ensure machine and attachment manufacturers handbook is available
  • Appropriate PPE to include safety footwear, non-snag outer clothing, gloves (min EN388 4.1.3.1)
  • Ensure hydraulic couplings are compatible, undamaged, and clean prior to connection
  • Operator to ensure system is not pressurised
  • De-pressurise couplings safely using correct procedures – check rams are not under load, only depress coupling valve lock away from operator avoiding contamination (PPE to be worn)
  • Do not check pressurised systems for leaks by hand
  • Ensure all operators are clear prior to pressurising a system
  • Ensure electrical couplings are compatible, undamaged, and clean prior to connection
  • Attachments are to be CE marked
133
Use of cutting head attachment1. Injuries from moving parts
2. Crush/trap injuries
3. Cuts, bruises, and contamination
4. Damage to plant
5. Struck by
6. Flying debris
7. Chain shot		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • Pre-use checks to be completed in accordance with manufacturer's instructions
  • Only timber shall be cut with the cutting attachment
  • Material should be processed from a safe and stable position
  • Material to be processed must be within the operating tolerances of the cutting head as stated by the manufacturer
  • Operators are to have access to both the cutting head and base unit operator's manual
  • No alterations or modifications are to be made to the cutting head without consent from the manufacturer
  • Material to be processed should be in a near to as upright position as possible
  • Material for processing must not hang over the operator's position
  • Processed material should be positioned where the base machine can safely reach
  • Material only to be felled away from or parallel to any overhead structure
  • A minimum of a 1m buffer zone should be maintained between the base unit and obstructions e.g. Fences
  • Operator to switch off machine after use
  • Do not place hands or feet inside moving components
  • Bystanders to maintain safety distances as specified by the manufacturer
  • Chain sharpening and bar maintenance carried out in accordance with manufacturers specification, including visual pre use checks
  • The operator shall conduct a test run of all functions prior as part of the starting procedure
  • Material to be cut in short sections, ensure no foreign material enters the saw unit
  • Observe the wind speed prior to cutting, wind speed that will have a direct affect over the control of what has been cut should result in work stopping
  • Regular bar maintenance shall be carried out to ensure oilways are clear, rails are straight and correct depth maintained
  • Sprocket wear is not to exceed manufacturers recommendations (approx. 0.6mm)
  • Pre use checks of the cutting head by the operator shall include a visual inspection of the chain for defects and to confirm it is safe for use
  • Ensure as part of pre use checks chain tension is correct
  • The cutting head is never to be pointed at people whilst running
  • The saw head shall be engaged as close to the timber being processed as possible
144
Use of a winch1. Crush or other injury type due to: (a) Failure of lifting accessory; (b) Incorrect slinging; (c) Incorrect use4416Operator
Work Team
  • All lifting equipment to have relevant thorough examination certification in order and available for inspection
  • All lifting equipment shall be clearly marked with a unique identification number and safe working load
  • All lifting equipment shall be subject to visual examination prior to and after use
  • All lifting equipment shall be protected from sharp edges
  • No piece of equipment shall be overloaded
  • Any hook used shall be of the "C" type or to be fitted with a safety device
  • When attached all lifting equipment to be checked for security prior to lifting/pulling
  • Correct capacity lifting and handling equipment to be used for slinging configurations
  • A trial lift may be carried out to assess the centre of gravity
  • Ensure hoist rope is reeved as per manufactures manual
144
Mobile Crane↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Placing or moving the crane1. Contact with personnel
2. Contact with proximity hazards		4416Work team
Client
General public
  • Driver to be qualified to place and operate the crane
  • A safe working zone must be established with the use of signs, cones and barrier tape
  • The Appointed Person shall maintain a safe working distance when assisting the operator and must remain vigilant to the presence of other people on or near the work site
  • All staff on site must maintain a safe distance from the crane and be aware of moving site traffic
  • All other persons and bystanders must be excluded from the safe working zone
  • A distance of 15m must be maintained from any cable on metal poles; A distance of 9m must be maintained from any cable on wooden poles
  • A distance of at least 600mm must be ensured between any other features likely to be contacted by any part of the crane. For example, slewing of the jib may create a shear hazard between the counterweights and a nearby building
  • Appointed person to maintain direct radio contact with driver or line of sight
144
Ground instability1. Overturn
2. Collapse
3. Bogging down
4. Damage to services		4416Work team
Client
General public
  • The operator and the Appointed Person must discuss and agree the most appropriate place to site the crane
  • The Appointed Person must conduct a thorough examination of the ground and surrounding area to confirm the existence (or not) of any sub-surface features. The property owner may be able to advise on the proximity of cellars and septic tanks
248
Personnel carried on crane1. Crush injuries
2. Falls from crane
3. Fatality		4520Staff
  • No person is to ride the machine to the worksite except the driver in the correct driving position
155
Falling debris from a suspended load1. Head injury
2. Crush
3. Fatality		4520Work team
Client
General public
  • All staff and bystanders close to the worksite are to maintain a 10m distance from the area beneath a suspended load whilst the load is in transit or suspended at height
  • If it is seen or suspected that any has wandered into the danger zone, the machine must be stopped and the person instructed that they are in a danger area. No work will commence until the visitor has moved to a place of safety
  • When supervised by the Appointed Person, staff may use ropes or poles to direct a load that is suspended in a low position to an appropriate place of rest. Under no circumstances must any person enter a position beneath the suspended load
  • All lifting equipment must have been subject to the inspection schedule laid down by the Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations
2510
Crane overload1. Structural collapse of crane – struck by
2. Failure of machinery – struck by
3. Fatality
4. Crush injury		4520Work team
Client
General public
Property
  • The Appointed Person must be a qualified arborist and must have experience of estimating the weight of tree sections. A deliberately small section shall be removed in the first instance in order that all other sections can be benchmarked against that lift
  • The Appointed Person must maintain line of sight or audible communications with the crane operator at all times
  • A safety factor must be considered when asking the operator the current lifting capacity at the current distance. For example, if the operator informs you that he/she can lift 5 tonnes at the current distance, the Appointed Person may choose to adopt a 5:1 factor of safety and remove a section estimated at weighing 1 tonne or less
  • Loads should not be subject to dynamic forces (dropping) or swinging. All loads should be lifted in a controlled manner
  • When lifting a load from a position on the ground, the Appointed Person shall conduct a check lift prior to movement of the load
2510
Use of the crane to lift, lower or suspend a climber1. Fall from height – fatality4520Climber
  • When lifting, lowering or suspending a person the crane is required to have been thoroughly examined within a period no greater than six months
  • The Appointed Person must ensure that the attachment of the climbing system to the crane is appropriate (primary and back up system in place)
  • All climbing equipment must have been subject to LOLER thorough examination
  • The climbing line must be bagged and clipped to the climbers harness in order that a trailing line does not become entangled leading to injury to the climber
  • The climbing line must be long enough to allow the climber to self rescue at any point
  • The proposed journey must be through clear space and well away from any proximity hazards
  • The specific risk assessment of the Appointed Person must justify the use of the crane in this way – it must only be done if it is the safest option available
155
Uncontrolled movement of load1. Crush or strike injury4520Work team
Client
General public
Property
  • Wind factors: Wind velocity meter fitted to crane; Wind chart/Beaufort scale provided; Stop work when wind speed exceeds acceptable tolerances from crane (generally 10m/s); Boom to be retracted/lowered when weather conditions or wind speed is unclear
  • General: Crane operator to keep load in sight; Clear and unambiguous communication method established between crane operator and ground crew/climber; No person is to stand under a suspended load, nor is a load lifted over persons; Sun visor to be fitted to crane to reduce glare; Groundcrew to wear hi-visibility clothing; Crane operator is to visually confirm that the landing zone is clear prior to setting down a load
2510
Operating crane in an unlit or poorly lit area1. Crush or strike injury4416Work team
Client
General public
Property
  • Lighting provided on boom, platform and sliding beams (outriggers)
144
Unauthorised access and operation of unsupervised mobile crane2. Damage to crane, plant or personnel4416Work team
Client
General public
Property
  • Crane to be under the full control of trained and authorised operators at all times
  • Where full control cannot be maintained, and load is to be removed and crane made into a safe condition
  • The crane operator is responsible for ensuring unauthorised access into the crane
144
Protective Fencing↑ Top
HazardHow harm may occurLSL×SWho is at riskGood practice / guidanceControl measures required for safe workingLSL×S
Erection of tree protection fencing1. Injury through manual handling of panels and feet
2. Puncture wounds from exposed wire ends
3. Pinch injury when tightening couplings
4. Dropping of hand tools onto feet
5. Slips and trips at ground level
6. Fence collapse
7. Unauthorised access into fenced off areas
8. Noise induced deafness
9. Vibration related injury
10. Wrist, upper limb injury
11. Contact with battery fluid
12. Electrical discharge – short circuit		4416Operator
Work Team
Public
  • All operators trained, authorised and where appropriate certificated
  • PPE worn and used correctly in accordance with manufacturers guidance – safety footwear, gloves
  • Only put block trays down two at a time then stand panel in them as placing too many could cause trip hazards
  • Never leave panels leaning or standing without being fixed into block trays
  • Check the condition of panels whilst erecting, discarding damaged panels
  • Ensure no mesh wires protrude from the fence as a potential hazard
  • Cutting of wire should only be undertaken when wearing gloves and eye protection using the appropriate tool
  • Ensure all are aware of correct lifting and handling methods; Share heavy and bulky loads
  • Arrange delivery of materials to site and as near to work areas as is practicable
  • Use mechanical aids to reduce all lifting and moving of material
  • Site to be walked prior to start of works to identify, mark or remove possible trip hazards
  • Above ground stabilising systems should include panels secured with a minimum of two couplers, set at least 1m apart and uniform along the fence line. The panels should be supported on the inner side by stabilizer struts, which should normally be attached to a base plate secured with ground pins. Where the fencing is to be erected on retained hard surfacing or it is otherwise unfeasible to use ground pins, e.g. due to the presence of underground services, the stabilizer struts should be mounted on a block tray
  • Where protective fencing consists of a vertical and horizontal scaffold framework, the vertical tubes should be spaced at a maximum interval of 3m and driven securely into the ground to a minimum depth of 0.6m, stabiliser struts may be secured via ground pins or additional vertical tubes
  • No fence is to be erected or left without stabiliser struts being installed and secure
  • All weather notices should be attached to the fence stating words such as "CONSTRUCTION EXCLUSION ZONE – NO ACCESS"
  • Use of tool belt or similar to store couplings, and hand tools when not in use
  • Breaking of ground only completed following the completion of CAT and GENNY scans or receipt of a permit to dig/break ground from client
  • Impact gun: The operator is to ensure they wear close fitting clothing to ensure a snag hazard isn't present; Pre use checks of the gun shall be carried out to determine it is safe for use; Protective footwear, ear protection, and gloves as minimum items of PPE are to be worn; Machinery shall be entered onto the noise and vibration asset register and operators informed of the risks associated with using such machinery; Machinery is to be operated with two hands, using the designated handles only; During use the operator should brace their stance; If the tool stalls, stop the machine to remove, do not attempt to pull at the item – Do not switch the device on again while the insertion tool is stalled; Be aware that bits may become hot during use; Use only batteries designed for the tool – do not use battery packs from other systems; Do not use this product in any other way as stated for normal use; To reduce the risk of fire, personal injury, and product damage due to a short circuit, never immerse the tool, battery pack or charger in fluid; Do not store the battery pack together with metal objects (short circuit risk); Remove the battery pack before starting any work on the machine
144
Arborite Tree Services Ltd  |  Common Factor Risk Assessments V9  |  13th June 2025  |  Due for review: 13th June 2026  |  Author: Chris Cooper-Abbs, HSEQ Advisor  |  Approved by: Jon Challinor, Managing Director

TBT-...
About Toolbox Talks
  1. A Toolbox Talk is an informal group discussion that focuses on a health & safety issue, carried out on an ad-hoc basis to promote a safety culture.
  2. They encourage everyone to explore the risks of specific issues and think about ways to deal with them.
  3. Anyone can present a TBT — you don't need to be an expert. Present clearly and take the process seriously.
  4. TBTs should cover issues identified from site walks, operational activity or business processes such as risk assessments or LOLER.
  5. This form does not need to record every detail — the key points are enough.
TBT Details
Date
Presented By
Topic / Title
Main Points of Discussion
Action Points
Employee Acknowledgements
Print Name
Signature
